Moreover, the stylistic analysis of drama is now more plentiful (cf Burton 1980, Culpeper 2001, Culpeper, Short and Verdonk 1998, Freeman forthcoming, Herman 1995). (To the lists of publications in the preceding sentence, we should add relevant contributions in the collections referred to towards the end of this paragraph.) Douthwaite (2000), which is devoted to foregrounding theory, uses many prose examples and analyses, and Simpson (2003) is an innovative account of satire which draws on stylistics, discourse analysis and pragmatics, and applies them to literary and non-literary texts. A very useful dictionary of stylistics (Wales 2000 [1989]) and a number of collections of stylistics articles (e.g. Bex, Burke and Stockwell 2000, Carter 1982, Carter and Simpson 1989, Freeman 1981, van Peer 1988, Short 1989, Toolan 1992 and Weber 1996) have been published, which give a representative flavour of the range and depth of the field. Finally, among other organisations, there is a flourishing international association, the Poetics and Linguistics Association (http://www.pala.ac.uk), which has stylistics at the centre of its interests. Moreover, there are more journals which concentrate on stylistics-related articles, of which Language and Literature, Style and the Journal of Literary Semantics are probably the most influential. Among journals relating to text and discourse, Text & Talk (formerly named Text) and The Journal of Pragmatics take stylistics articles relevant to their areas of concentration. Language and Literature (a key journal for our field) and The Year's Work in English Studies carry annual summaries of recent work published in stylistics, and Style also presents occasional annotated bibliographies of relevant areas.
There have also been useful advances in neighbouring areas of study which are important to stylistics. Linguistic pragmatics (including politeness analysis and discourse analysis) is much more developed than it was in 1981, leading to more sophisticated stylistic work on fiction and drama in particular. Indeed, the use of pragmatics has become so prevalent in stylistics since the early 1980s that it would be fair to say that stylistics has
283
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
undergone a ‘pragmatic turn’, which started around the time we were writing the first edition of Style in Fiction. In addition, other sub-disciplines have sprung up which overlap to some degree with the aims of stylistics. The field of narratology (e.g. Bal 1997 [1985], Bonheim 1982, 1990, Chatman 1978, Fludernik 1996, Herman 2002, 2003, Prince 1982, Rimmon-Kenan 2002 [1983], Ronen 1994, Ryan 1991, Stanzel 1984, van Peer and Chatman 2001) is probably the most notable for stylisticians interested in the study of prose fiction, as it has contributed much to the understanding of viewpoint, plot structure and fictional worlds, areas in which stylistics has long had an interest.
Another, more general, area worthy of note is what is usually called 'empirical literary studies'. Of course stylistics itself is empirical, in the sense that it attempts to analyse texts as accurately and objectively as possible, taking into account what we know about textual structure and the nature of language processing. Empirical literary studies, on the other hand, undertake analyses of informant responses of the kind psychologists typically perform. This is done to see what empirical evidence there is to support or disconfirm particular accounts of reader reactions to individual texts, and so to understand more fully the processes by which literary texts are interpreted (cf Bortolussi and Dixon 2003, Emmott, Sanford and Morrow 2006, Gerrig 1993, Hakemulder 2000, Zwaan 1993). The aims of empirical literary studies thus also overlap with those of stylistics, and stylistics is in a good position to provide detailed interpretative accounts to be tested out by those involved in empirical literary studies. Indeed, some academics (e.g. van Peer 1986) perform both stylistic analysis and informant work.
Although mostificantities have focused on the analysis of literary texts, many have also extended their interest to the analysis of non-literary texts too (e.g. Cook 1992, Freeborn 1996). Not surprisingly, then, there has been a general overlap between the work of stylisticians and those interested in language varieties and the study of register. Another related approach is that which now most frequently goes under the title 'critical discourse analysis' (CDA), although the term 'critical linguistics' has also sometimes been used to refer to our own area of study. CDA shares a considerable number of analytical tools with stylistics, though it is more closely associated with the linguistic school of M.A.K. Halliday (cf, e.g., Halliday and Matthiessen 2004) than the more eclectic stylistics enterprise with which this book is associated. The defining difference of CDA is that it analyses (usually non-literary) texts in order to expose what the authors usually see as unjustified socio-political assumptions encoded non-obviously in the text concerned (e.g. Fairclough 1989, 1995, 2000, 2003; Fowler 1991, Wodak and Meyer 2001). In this respect it has interesting correlations with the deconstructive and postmodernist styles of analysis practised by some modern literary theorists. CDA approaches to the stylistic analysis of literary texts can be seen in Birch (1989, 1991) and Weber (1992). Although, in principle, CDA could be applied to any text and from any ideological
284
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
perspective, there is a tendency for it to be practised by those with a left-wing political persuasion, and the stylistician Henry Widdowson has criticised critical discourse analysts for, among other things, effectively knowing what interpretation they are going to 'impose' on a text before they have even read it (see Widdowson 1995b, 1996, 2000 and Fairclough 1996, with whom Widdowson has debated the issue).
A fast-developing area which is having an increasing effect on contemporary stylistics is corpus linguistics (cf Biber 1988, Biber, Conrad and Reppen 1998, McEnery and Wilson 1996, Sinclair 2004, Thomas and Short 1996). Although, in its earlier years, corpus linguistics set itself the task of creating large and varied corpora of (extracts from) a wide range of written and spoken texts in order to investigate central aspects of language like lexis and grammar, of late we have seen the growth of corpus applications to literature. Louw (1993, 1997) has used large corpora of English to establish what he calls the typical 'semantic prosody' of particular words, i.e. the typical contexts in which particular words occur, as a way of establishing the generalised associations we have for a word, and whether its semantic prosody in a particular text parallels, or contrasts with, its more typical semantic prosody. This technique is particularly useful in the study of poetry, but can also be used effectively with texts from other literary genres. In the remainder of this paragraph, we mention publications illustrating a wide range of applications of corpus-based techniques to literary style. Hori (2004) examines collocational patterns in the novels of Charles Dickens, Hoover (1999) uses the British National Corpus (BNC) and a specially-constructed corpus of novels to test the claims that Halliday (1971) made about Golding's The Inheritors (see pp. 25–6), Hardy (2003) combines a corpus approach with qualitative stylistic analysis to elucidate style and thematic world-view concerns in the fiction of Flannery O'Connor, and Stubbs (2005) discusses the benefits of a corpus-based approach for stylistics, using Conrad's Heart of Darkness as an example. Semino and Short (2004) use a manually tagged corpus to develop more accurate and complete models of discourse presentation in literary and non-literary narrative (for further details see section 11.3). Others are developing a corpus-based approach to the study of metaphor (see the 2001 special issue of Language and Literature 11, 1) in order to see how exactly linguistic recognition of metaphor in texts can be related to the conceptual constructs of cognitive metaphor theory associated with Lakoff and others (e.g. Lakoff and Johnson 1980, Lakoff and Turner 1989, Turner 1996).
The use of relatively sophisticated computational techniques in corpus linguistics might lead to fears that the computer is ‘taking over’ the domain of literary language in a way that leaves little scope for individual response and interpretation. This fear would be unfounded, however, as corpus techniques are (at least at present) incapable of simulating the human capacity to understand and interpret. The contributions of corpus
285
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
linguistics can be put more positively as follows: it allows us to carry out fast, exhaustive and accurate searches of texts, to undertake certain kinds of quantitative processing, including the comparison of different texts in terms of key vocabulary, and so on. The computer remains the servant of the human mind and sensibility. Procedures such as contextual concordance searches and frequency counts can be carried out on easily recognisable features of texts, such as vocabulary items, collocations and simple grammatical categories, but for more abstract levels of analysis (such as that of discourse presentation categories mentioned above), the human analyst has to annotate the text manually. The 'corpus turn' in stylistics, if it can be called such, is partly due to the fact that an enormous range of literary texts in English are now available in electronic form, and basic processing tasks can be carried out with relatively little difficulty on the entire oeuvre of major writers of the past, such as Shakespeare, Austen, James and Dickens.
The 'cognitive turn' within linguistics is, if anything, a more substantial influence, applying to much more than metaphor (see Ungerer and Schmid (forthcoming) for a useful introduction to work in the field). The mental spaces and blending theory of Fauconnier (1985), for example, has been developed so that it applies to literature as well as language more generally, and is becoming more influential in stylistics. As before, we find a wide range of applications to literature of a fast developing research paradigm. Fauconnier and Turner (2002) include chapters giving blending theory accounts of fictional worlds and of character portrayal. Turner (1996) sees the 'literary mind' as central to language, its origins and development. A recent issue of Language and Literature ((15), 1, 2006) is devoted to blending theory accounts of literary texts, and its editor has also used the theory to examine travel narratives by Jonathan Raban (Dancygier, 2005). Just as the 'cognitive turn' within linguistics has become more general, so has the 'cognitive turn' within stylistics. Stockwell (2002) provides a useful introductory account, and Semino and Culpeper (2002) and Gavins and Steen (2003) give an indication of the range of work in what is increasingly being known as 'cognitive stylistics'. An area which is clearly relevant to viewpoint analysis is 'deictic shift theory', which tries to explain how readers move perceptually from one deictic centre to another as they read (for example, from their own deictic centre to that of a character in a novel, and from that character's deictic centre to another's, or to the same character's different deictic centre in a 'flashback' within the novel). See papers in Duchan, Bruder and Hewitt (1995) and Stockwell (2002, pp. 46–55).
It is not just that new academic areas within literary studies and linguistics have grown up around stylistics, giving it new things to relate to. To some degree, since 1981, stylistics itself has also changed what it concentrates on, and in ways which have made the name 'stylistics' more difficult
286
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
to live with. Style in Fiction begins with two chapters devoted almost entirely to the elucidation of the notion of 'style'. This was because although the stylistics of poetry was concerned rather more with meaning, the study of prose had been dominated up to 1980 by the issues of (a) how to identify and account for style in general and (b) how to characterise and differentiate the styles of particular authors. But things were already changing in 1981: this can be seen in the way that Chapter 3 of Style in Fiction uses linguistic analysis to exemplify not only style, but also meaning and effect, in the three short story openings examined. Since then, the study of authorial style has moved away from the mainstream. (However, the corpus stylistics approach referred to above is currently leading to a revival of interest, while authorship detection studies, using computational methods, have continued throughout our period, as is demonstrated by an examination of back numbers of the Journal of Literary and Linguistic Computing.) The mainstream stylisiticians have concentrated much more on trying to explain how, when we read, we get from the words on the page to the meanings in our heads and effects in our hearts. The result has been that 'stylistics' no longer transparently describes what most people who call themselves stylisiticians do. There have been various attempts to change the name of the enterprise to, for example, 'discourse stylistics', 'critical linguistics', 'literary pragmatics' or 'literary linguistics'. But because stylistics covers such a wide range of analytical activities (including the stylistic analysis of non-literary texts), none of the alternatives seem properly to fit the bill either. So although the term 'stylistics' no longer appears to represent the area of study very accurately, it is still in fairly common use, and we are happy to live with it.
As a sub-discipline, stylistics sits athwart the boundary between linguistic and literary study, and sitting on a fence always has its drawbacks. One obvious such drawback is that those in the middle of the two big fields which the fence connects/divides may not even know you are there. Many mainstream descriptive and theoretical linguists and almost all of the literary critical community are unaware of stylistics or else studiously ignore it, even though it has useful things to say to both groups. Detailed analyses of texts often point up interesting problems for particular descriptive and theoretical accounts of language, and detailed analyses of particular texts have an obvious relevance for literary scholars studying the relevant authors or periods. But the ignorance of neighbouring colleagues may still be preferable to outright hostility, which has also been a mark of the relations between stylistics and the major academic subjects it connects to. The pug-nacious 'debate' between Roger Fowler and the literary critic F.W. Bateson (the contributions to which are collected together in Fowler, 1971) formed a backdrop to our original writing of Style in Fiction, and Bateson's attack on the stylistics enterprise has since been repeated by a number of others. For contributions to the 'debate' begun by Stanley Fish (1996 [1973]), see Toolan
287
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
(1996 [1990]), Hoover (1999, pp. 19–52) and Stubbs (2005). For a similar debate initiated by Mackay (1996), see Short et al. (1998), Mackay (1999) and Short and van Peer (1999).
Although these border disputes clear the air from time to time, they never seem to change the minds of those engaged in them. One of us who has been involved in the most recent such debate has found it a rather fruitless experience. There is a tendency for the proponents in such debates to attack straw men, by giving outdated accounts of the other side (for example, the common accusation that modern stylistics is 'formalist' in spite of the fact that it makes considerable use of informal pragmatic inference and makes reference to psychological processing in its accounts). Hence although we have provided references above for those who want to follow up on these debates, we would prefer to get on with what we enjoy and do best; analysing texts and exploring particular descriptive and theoretical issues (of which there are plenty to examine).
The last general area we would like to comment on in this section is that of pedagogy. If stylistics is to be of any practical benefit, it must be because it helps others to understand and appreciate particular texts in detail and to perform their own analyses of other texts. For this reason, the needs of students have never been far from the thoughts of most stylisticians. Style in Fiction, with its detailed and exemplificatory textual analyses, was always intended to be a useful book for students as well as a contribution to the academic study of language in literature. This can be seen not only in its analysis of extended pieces of text, but in the provision of passages for further discussion at the end of the book and in the development of a checklist of linguistic and stylistic categories at the end of Chapter 3. This checklist was designed to help students become more systematic and detailed in their own analyses, and it is an approach which has been developed further in Short (1996). Since the publication of Style in Fiction we have seen a number of introductory books on stylistics. Bradford (1997), Fowler (1996 [1986]), Short (1996), Simpson (1997) and Toolan (1997) are all textbooks which cover the stylistic analysis of all three literary genres, and Simpson (2004) is a cross-genre, activity-based introduction which also contains readings from works by other stylisticians. A number of scholars have developed stylistics-related pedagogical approaches to the integration of language and literature teaching, both in mother-tongue and second/foreign language learning contexts (e.g. Brumfit and Carter 1986, Carter and McRae 1996, Short 1989 and Widdowson 1995a). This pedagogical focus has also been seen in scholarly journals: Style (37, 1, 2003) is devoted to stylistics pedagogy and Language and Literature (15, 3, 2006) is a special issue on Short's freely-available web-based introductory stylistics course (http://www.lancs.ac.uk/fass/projects/stylistics/start.htm) together with a series of pedagogical investigations worldwide on student reactions in using it.
288
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
11.2 New developments in the stylistic analysis of prose fiction and what, with hindsight, we would add to Style in Fiction
Let us assume that the general aim of stylistics is an attempt to explain how readers get from the words of a text to (a) an understanding of it and (b) a felt response to it. We can then see that a large part of what is involved in the enterprise with respect to prose fiction texts is the attempt to connect textual analysis and processes of reader inference to the important general non-linguistic literary critical concepts which, when taken together, comprise the understanding of, and response to, novels and stories. We probably cannot produce an exhaustive list of these non-linguistic critical concepts, but the following would surely have to be included and given a prominent place: (i) style, (ii) point of view, (iii) story/plot, (iv) fictional worlds, (v) character, (vi) theme and (vii) evaluation and appreciation. When Style in Fiction was written we concentrated mainly on (i)-(ii). We did not ignore (iii)-(vi), and indeed Chapter 5 was devoted to language in relation to fictional worlds. But 'story', 'plot', 'character' and 'characterisation', 'theme' and 'evaluation' did not get references in the index, let alone chapters or sections devoted to them, even though it could be argued that the analyses of particular passages often elucidated character and theme, and suggested indirectly some of the reasons why the texts concerned should be highly valued. The book was organised around the concept of style, with considerable stretches (including Chapters 6, 8 and 10 and significant parts of Chapters 5 and 9) being devoted to linguistic aspects of viewpoint. This restriction is not surprising, given the state of knowledge at the time the book was written. A little was known about the linguistics of plot and the creation of fictional worlds, but hardly anything was known about how readers construe character when they read, and, even now, relatively little has been written about theme and evaluation in the stylistics tradition (although we note Short and Semino (forthcoming) on evaluation, and Louwerse and van Peer (2002) on theme).
If we were writing Style in Fiction now, it is clear from what we have said in section 11.1 that we would need to take deictic shift theory into account when discussing viewpoint, and corpus stylistics when discussing style. In addition, we would add major chapters on story/plot and character/ characterisation, elaborate and re-cast the chapter on the fictional world, and also be more explicit about the relationship of our textual analyses to theme and evaluation.
11.2.1 Story/plot
When Style in Fiction was first published, Anglo-American literary criticism had tended to ignore story and plot, largely because of an assumption that
289
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
story and plot, unlike style, were uninteresting to literary scholarship. As E.M. Forster (1974 [1927], pp. 40–41) had influentially put it:
… Yes – oh dear yes – the novel tells a story. That is the fundamental aspect without which it could not exist. That is the highest factor common to all novels, and I wish that it was not so, that it could be something different – melody, or perception of the truth, not this low atavistic form.
For the more we look at the story (the story that is a story, mind) the more we disentangle it from the finer growths that it supports, the less shall we find to admire. It runs like a backbone – or may I say a tapeworm, for its beginning and end are arbitrary...
But by the 1960s structuralist and formalist influences from outside the English-speaking world had brought a new interest in story structure. The rise of structuralism in French literary studies was strongly influenced by the work of the anthropologist Lévi-Strauss (e.g. Lévi-Strauss 1963). From Russian formalism came Propp's Morphology of the Folktale (published in Russian in 1928, but, until the late 1960s, virtually inaccessible in the West). By the time we wrote Style in Fiction, Lévi-Strauss believed that the structure of myths was connected inextricably to their role, as he saw it, of mediating the contradictory assumptions held by particular societies; and he saw these oppositions as being represented in stories by, among other things, oppositions between different kinds of events in the myths. This re-connected story and plot structure with overall textual meaning in an interesting way (see Hendricks (1972) for a stimulating application of this approach to a story by Ambrose Bierce). In a parallel development, Propp had shown that narrative genres (in particular Russian folktales) could be distinguished by a sophisticated analysis of the event-structures of the stories. The structuralist tradition explored, in a number of seminal works, the 'grammar' of stories, using analogies with the structures and derivations of sentences in generative grammar. Indeed, before the publication of Style in Fiction, influential works by Prince (1973) and Todorov (1969) had included the word 'grammar' in their titles. Other influences in the structural analysis of story and plot were Barthes (1977 [1966]), Chatman (1969), Brémond (1973) and Labov and Waletsky (1967). Since the early structuralist work a number of influential books devoted to narrative, Bal (1997 [1985]), Chatman (1978), Fludernik (1996), Herman (2002), Prince (1982), Ricoeur (1983), Rimmon-Kenan (2002) and Toolan (2001 [1988]) have all given space to the discussion of story structure, and other works have been specifically devoted to the technical study of plot, e.g. Dry (1981, 1983) and Pavel (1985). But, by and large, in stylistics the interest in such work has waned since, because the 'structuralist' approach was too formalistic or reductive to deliver results sensitive enough to account for readers' perceptions of what was happening in stories in literary texts.
290
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
Although interest in the analysis of story and plot has waned in stylistics, it is clearly of great importance for future developments of the field, and this is why we devote some space to it here. The key to success will probably involve an amalgam of (i) structuralist approaches, (ii) more inferential accounts derived from the work done in linguistic pragmatics and cognitive stylistics over the last twenty years, and (iii) foregrounding theory. An influential narratologist, David Herman (2002) has argued persuasively that work on what he calls 'story logic' (which involves character and viewpoint as well as story) needs to be based on inference by readers, which in effect will connect narratology, including structural narratology, to pragmatics and cognitive linguistics.
It would appear that our ability to be able to 'see' story structure 'through' the sentences of a text comes about through a combination of, or interaction between, what the psychologists call 'bottom-up' and 'top-down' processing. Roland Barthes (1977 [1966]) and W.O. Hendricks (1973a) tried to identify which clause-types typically contribute to the onward movement of stories. The action theory associated with Teun van Dijk (see below) is also highly relevant here, as is the work of discourse analysts like Michael Hoey (1983, 1991) working mainly on the structure of non-literary texts. Not surprisingly, there is a tendency for sentences and clauses which have (i) dynamic transitive verbs and (ii) main characters as subject or object to be important in plot development. Indeed, Fowler (1977, pp. 23–25), following Greimas, suggested that the constructional principles of narratives and sentences are the same, just as more recent work in blending theory also tends to assume an analogical 'fit' between basic linguistic structures and a wide range of cognitive phenomena. But it is not that difficult to find (a) examples of such sentences which are not important in the development of story in a particular text, and (b) 'descriptive' sentences which do turn out to be crucially important. For example, 'John kissed his wife', although it is a transitive structure with human agents, is unlikely to be an important action in driving a story forward precisely because it is a prototypical (and so backgrounded) action for husbands to perform, and so will not carry much 'story weight' unless it is marked within a particular story as being significant in some way. Similarly, although sentences like 'The door was open' and 'The room was full of wonderful things' are stative descriptions of the kind which do not prototypically push a story forward, it is not too difficult to invent a context where they could mark important story transitions. Imagine, for example, that some explorers had been trying, without success, to open a door in an ancient Egyptian tomb. Then, when they arrive the next day, 'The door was open. The room was full of wonderful things.' The first of the two state descriptions indicates a significant change of state in the world of the fiction, and the second state description allows us to infer (a) that the characters have finally managed to enter the room and (b) that what they have found is likely to be significant
291
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
in plot terms. In this sort of way, structuralist, pragmatic and foregrounding accounts could be interestingly allied in careful textual analysis. A completely adequate account will also need to take into account the effects of schematic assumptions on the interpretation of utterances, and the findings of action theory (van Dijk 1977, pp. 167–188, 1981, pp. 120–145, Kintsch and van Dijk 1983). Van Dijk points out that even apparently simple actions are complex, consisting of ‘... three phases: the mental conditions (wishes, desires, decisions, purposes, intentions – of which the internal structure is still pretty obscure, and irrelevant for our discussion), the actual doing, and the consequences of the doing (purposed or not): state, event or process’ (1981, p. 120). Pragmatically, it is possible to represent an action by stating the consequence of that action (cf the ‘door was open’ example above) or the prior intention of the relevant actor (cf ‘He decided to open the door’) without describing the action itself. Moreover, simple actions are in turn linked in sequences, and are sub-parts of higher-level compound or complex actions.
For example, when George kills his simpleton friend, Lennie, near the end of Steinbeck's Of Mice and Men because he does not want the pursuing lynch mob to get Lennie first (see the discussion of the examples from this novel in sections 6.1 and 7.5.1 for some of the context leading up to the lynch mob's blood lust), the description of the act of shooting Lennie is broken down for dramatic, foregrounded and slow-motion effect into simpler sub-parts, the pointing of the gun at the back of Lennie's head, and so on. And the fact that George raises and lowers the gun more than once helps us to infer the battle inside his mind between (a) his wish/intention to shoot Lennie when he is not looking in order to save him from of dying in terror at the hands of the lynch mob and (b) his wish/obligation not to be the instrument of his friend's death (we will return to textual 'worlds', such as these, in section 11.2.2 below).
The quotations below are extracted from the last three pages of the novel. In the intervening text, the lynch mob is getting closer and closer. To ensure Lennie dies happy, George tells him the details of their shared dream (a dream which he has repeatedly re-told throughout the novel) of having a small farm of their own. Meanwhile, Lennie, sitting in front of him, looks out over the Salinas valley:
He reached in his side pocket and brought out Carlson's Luger; he snapped off the safety, and the hand and the gun lay on the ground behind Lennie's back. He looked at the back of Lennie's head, at the place where the spine and skull were joined....
...George raised the gun and his hand shook, and he dropped his hand to the ground again....
...George looked down at the gun....
292
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
... The voices came close now. George raised the gun and listened to the voices.
Lennie begged, 'Le's do it now. Le's get that place now.'
'Sure, right now. I gotta. We gotta.'
And George raised the gun and steadied it, and he brought the muzzle of it close to the back of Lennie's head. The hand shook violently, but his face set and his hand steadied. He pulled the trigger. The crash of the shot rolled up the hills and rolled down again. Lennie jarred, and then settled slowly forward to the sand, and he lay without quivering.
[John Steinbeck, Of Mice and Men, Chapter VI]
The suspense is created here in two ways. First, Steinbeck pulls apart textually (a) the preliminary and intentional phases and (b) the performance phase of the sub-parts of the macro-action. Second, he describes George trying, and failing, to perform the terrible deed. Then, when the lynch mob are almost upon them, George finally manages it. The dramatic irony of the ambiguity of the pronoun 'it' in Lennie's final request and the ellipsis in George's reply ('I gotta. We gotta.') increase the pathos of the scene, and Steinbeck creates more suspense at the very end of the description by telling us of the echoing sound of the gun before he describes the effect of the shot on Lennie himself. Even then, he does not tell us straightforwardly that Lennie is dead, but of course it is inferred. Here we see how foregrounding, action theory, and the inferencing of cognitive theory and pragmatics come together.
So far we have used the terms 'story' and 'plot' more or less interchangeably. This is in spite of the fact that critics usually think of plots as being more interesting critically than stories, and so we should explain our usage. The critical attitude just referred to probably derives from E.M. Forster (1974 [1927]), where he famously suggested that plots, unlike stories, exhibited relations of cause and effect. His examples were:
Story: 'The king died and then the queen died.'
Plot: 'The king died and then the queen died of grief.'
Forster says that the plot example has cause and effect, and this is clearly specified in the adverbial phrase in the second sentence. But the problem with the opposition he proposes, and which has held so much sway in the world of Anglo-American literary criticism, is that, in spite of what Forster says, it is difficult not to construct a cause and effect relation between the two sentences of the 'story' example too. This will be seen more obviously if we change Forster's example slightly to:
'The king died and then the queen cried.'
293
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
Although there is no overt connection, readers will infer one, using the pragmatic maxim of relation (Grice 1975) or the principle of relevance (Sperber and Wilson 1986). It is just about possible to imagine that the queen cried for no reason at all, or for some reason not referred to in the text (perhaps she was peeling onions?), but the likeliest inference, given our schematic knowledge about deaths in families, is that she cried because the king died. Some story structures are clearly more basic than others. For example, if we are told at the beginning of a story what is going to happen at, or near, the end, we will relate that re-ordered presentation of the events to the order in which they 'must have occurred' chronologically (the fabula in Russian Formalist terms), and perceive the achronological re-ordering (the sjuizhet in Russian Formalist terms) as significant in our understanding of the text. For example, Marabou Stork Nightmares by Irvine Welsh begins with the first-person narrator in hospital, in a drug-induced coma. Most of the remainder of the novel is his narration of events earlier in his life, which are read as an account of how he ended up in the coma, as well as explaining why, at the end of the novel, he is brutally murdered while still in his coma. As with the sentences 'The door was open. The room was full of wonderful things.' above, the significance of cause-and-effect relations and unusual orderings in the presentation of events seems to need careful pragmatic and foregrounding analysis.
We came very close to adding a chapter on the analysis of story and plot to the first edition of Style in Fiction. Indeed, we even performed initial analyses, which we have never published, on 'The Bucket and the Rope' by T.F. Powys (which we now discuss in Chapter 12) and part of 'The Short Happy Life of Francis Macomber' by Ernest Hemingway. But Style in Fiction was already considerably longer than we had intended, story analysis did not sit easily with the structure or title of our book, and, in spite of our interest in it, we were not convinced that we had enough analytical tools at our disposal to provide a satisfying account in the kind of detail we had provided elsewhere in the volume. But 25 years on, the possibility of combining structural, pragmatic and foregrounding analysis in the study of stories and story-telling looks like an area ripe for development.
11.2.2 Fictional worlds, text worlds, mental spaces
There have been interesting developments since 1981 in the study of how, when we read fiction, we infer, and operate interpretatively with, the fictional world 'in' or 'behind' the text (see Pavel 1986, for instance). In narratology, there have been advances within what is often called 'possible worlds theory', the study of how the main world of the fiction 'interacts' with other, closely related, 'possible worlds', for example the 'belief worlds' or 'wish worlds' of particular characters. Clashes between the various possible worlds (e.g. the wish of a character to change the status quo) can
294
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
effectively drive the plot of a story forward and at the same time help us to infer contrasting and/or parallel relations among characters. Interesting exponents of this possible-worlds approach are Eco (1990), Ryan (1991) and Ronen (1994), though this narratological work could be usefully connected more explicitly to the detailed linguistic structure of the texts considered. Semino (1997) concentrates mainly on fictional worlds in poems, but her approach sits much more clearly within the stylistics tradition and could easily be extended to other genres. She interestingly combines, in one approach to the study of fictional worlds, three different kinds of analysis: (a) the narratological possible-worlds approach of Ryan (1991), (b) the psychological approach of schema theory, and (c) the linguistic-stylistic approach, and in particular the study of deixis. It is clear that as stylistics develops it is moving to integrate linguistic, psychological and more literary approaches, and Semino's work is an excellent exemplar of this.
Another influential approach to the study of fictional worlds is that associated with Paul Werth and his followers, and perhaps best seen in Werth (1999). Hidalgo Downing (2000) uses Werth's approach, among others, in a useful study of Catch 22 by Joseph Heller, and Gavins (2003) uses it to analyse Donald Barthelme's Snow White. The term Werth uses is 'text world' rather than 'fictional world', as he is concerned with inference into worlds 'behind' any text, fictional or non-fictional. But a number of his examples are from well-known fictional texts, and his approach to 'text world construction', although it is by no means perfect, and was still under development when he died, is admirable in its focus on charting in considerable detail how readers get from the precise linguistic configuration of a text, via processes of inference, to the world evinced by that text. This line of research looks most promising, and, if we were trying to write a 'perfect and complete' Style in Fiction now, we would almost certainly want to add a chapter on the inference of fictional worlds, possible worlds related to the world of the fiction, and consequent meanings and effects, thus building substantially on what we said about fictional worlds in Chapter 5.
One of the difficulties at the present time is that the accounts of story, action theory, possible worlds and text worlds, which have arisen in different areas of study, overlap with one another, and it is not entirely clear how these approaches can be successfully integrated. Moreover, the mental spaces and blending theory referred to in section 11.1 arguably has the potential to subsume all of these approaches (and indeed the theories of character we describe in section 11.2.3, and much more besides) within its scope. All this raises two issues which will need considerable future research to resolve. First, although theories of general applicability are very attractive in principle, their very generality tends to mean that they become less explanatory in a detailed way when applied to the more particular phenomena of individual texts. So it is not clear, for example, how useful it will be to subsume
295
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
the other action and worlds theories within the more general mental spaces theory. Second, the action theory, fictional worlds and mental spaces accounts are attractive in literary critical terms because they seem to relate directly to critical concerns like story, fictional worlds and character. But they all have to be 'worked out' in detail on a range of texts before we can be sure that the approaches are reliably replicable from analyst to analyst. Even the text worlds approach, which is perhaps the best connected of all to the linguistic details of textual structure, still has a long way to go before it achieves the detailed analytical precision and reliability associated with more traditional forms of stylistic analysis.
11.2.3 Character and characterisation
Although, by and large, character and characterisation are not addressed as a major organisational topic in Style in Fiction (but see section 5.4.3, 'Speech and character') the book contains plenty of analyses which could easily be re-cast as discussions of characterisation. For example, the discussion of speech acts in a passage from Pride and Prejudice (see pp. 234–6) is framed to elucidate the conversational behaviour of Mr and Mrs Bennet in one particular interaction. But the conversational behaviour of the two characters in the relevant passage is also reflective of their behaviour throughout the novel and so can easily be seen as indicating their individual characterisations and their character-relations. Similarly, Chapter 6 on mind style, as well the various discussions on different aspects of viewpoint, is clearly relevant to the portrayal of character. At the time we wrote the original book, characterisation was not very high on the agenda of prose fiction study in stylistics, or criticism more generally. Perhaps because of the dominance of the critical discussion of viewpoint in prose fiction, the study of characterisation in twentieth-century criticism was, by and large, more evident in the criticism of drama than that of prose fiction, though it was not very plentiful even there.
The history of the study of characterisation in literary studies is somewhat chequered. Until the twentieth century, there was a tendency in drama criticism, seen most famously in Bradley (1904) and still evident in less sophisticated discussions of characters in fiction, to think of characters as if they were real people, with existences both on stage and off stage. This approach was debunked in the seminal essay 'How many children had Lady Macbeth?' by L.C. Knights (1963 [1946]), which helped to move Anglo-American drama criticism away from the study of character towards the study of textual properties. But reactions from one fallacy often go to the extreme of the opposite fallacy – as is seen in Weinsheimer (1979), who viewed the idea that characters were people-like as completely irrelevant to the criticism of fiction: 'Emma Woodhouse is not a woman nor need be described as if it were.' But this kind of extreme statement seems to fly in
296
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
the face of what we know about the reading process. Just as readers infer a fictional world 'behind' the text when they read, they also furnish that fictional world with characters as well as other objects, and, not surprisingly, they use their knowledge of, and schematic assumptions about, people in the real world when construing characters in novels. Hence the use of the analysis of conversational behaviour as evidence for characterisation, as in the above-mentioned example from Pride and Prejudice (and elsewhere in stylistics) seems helpful, as it is not unlike how we construe personality from real-world conversations. As with literary criticism, most of the stylistic work on characterisation so far has been on dramatic texts, but it is not difficult to apply the type of analysis developed for the stylistics of drama to character interaction in prose texts. The essays by Bennison, Culpeper, Herman, Lowe and Toolan in Culpeper, Short and Verdonk (1998) are all directly relevant to characterisation, as is much of Herman (1995) and Chapters 7 and 8 of Short (1996). Chapter 5 of Rimmon-Kenan (2002 [1983]), Chapter 4 of Toolan (2001 [1988]) and Chapter 11 of Toolan (1990) are on character in the novel, and Person (1999), which is devoted to conversation in fiction, is also relevant. Chapter 5 of Herman (2002) is a suggestive update of narratological accounts of character and Chapter 12 of Fauconnier and Turner (2002) gives an outline of a mental spaces approach.
The most highly articulated stylistic account of character in fiction to date is Culpeper (2001). Although it focuses on drama, this is a book which is likely to become highly influential in stylistic discussion of character in the discussion of the novel. Culpeper brings together a 'top-down' approach to character, based mainly on social cognition and attribution theory within psychology, and the more 'bottom-up' approach seen in much stylistic analysis (which concentrates on particular textual detail and how character and characterisation can be inferred from that textual detail). This approach enables Culpeper to chart a theoretical course between the critical extremes of Bradley on the one hand and Weinsheimer on the other, and to connect detailed analysis of conversational behaviour with social constructs via linguistic inference and schematic approaches to character types. Culpeper's work thus effectively enables us to relate the text-analytical work on character interaction already extant in prose stylistics to the principled elucidation of character and characterisation. Besides working directly within the stylistics tradition, Culpeper also connects his work to useful modern critical accounts of characterisation (e.g. Margolin 1989, 1990 and Pfister 1988) and historical linguistic-stylistic work on characterisation in early literature (e.g. Taavitsainen 1999). He takes into account not just the behaviour of particular characters, but also what other characters say about them (which could, in turn, be linked to narrator statements about characters in prose fiction, although different kinds of narrators will produce some complications). He also (a) distinguishes between behavioural factors which can be character-related and those
297
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
which need to be 'discounted' because they are contextually determined, and (b) shows how Taavitsainen's notion of 'surge features' can be used analytically to account for why particular stretches of character talk and behaviour are especially relevant to perceptions of character. Interestingly, he also points out that in fictions, unlike the real world, once we have read the whole text we have all the behaviour available on which to base judgments about character, and so can come to a properly 'rounded' judgment not easily possible in real life. Not surprisingly, if Style in Fiction was being written now there would be a substantial discussion of character and characterisation in the novel, building on the recent work of Culpeper and others, including Culpeper (2002), which outlines a possible cognitive stylistic approach to characterisation. We would need to explore not just a Culpeper-style analysis of how characters talk about themselves and others, and exhibit their character through what they say, but also how we can take the statements about characters by different kinds of narrators into account and how viewpoint blending between a narrator and particular characters would affect the perception of character.
11.3 New developments in the stylistics of prose fiction and what, with hindsight, we would change in Style in Fiction
We would like to feel that much of what we wrote 25 years ago has stood the test of time. We would want to stand, by and large, by our discussions of particular textual extracts, and where we disagree with our former selves, it would require too much detailed discussion than we have available here to examine what we would want to alter with respect to individual texts, and why. So below we will concentrate on more theoretical aspects of the stylistic description of prose fiction. We would also want to stand, by and large, by our general account of style (with the additional consideration of corpus-based works, as indicated in section 11.2, for example, in determining norms of style), and its exemplification through particular analyses in Chapters 1–4 of the original book.
However, there has been considerable attention to the stylistics of viewpoint in its widest sense, and the findings in this area, as well as developments in our own thinking, would lead us to various rather more substantial changes in the later chapters of the book, in addition to adding the work on deictic shift theory referred to in section 11.2.
11.3.1 Different kinds of viewpoint and different linguistic indicators of viewpoint
Our first edition made a distinction between (i) fictional point of view, that is the viewpoint held by one or more characters whose consciousness is
298
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
represented through the fiction, and (ii) discoursal point of view, that is the relationship between the teller (the 'implied author' or narrator and the fiction being represented). This distinction helped us to be clear about whose viewpoint was being represented and how viewpoints were sometimes blended. We also indicated some of the ways in which viewpoint could be indicated linguistically, but we did not provide a very systematic account of the linguistic features typically involved in viewpoint manipulation or of the different aspects of the notion of viewpoint that are involved when a person's point of view is represented. Now we would want to be more discriminating in both these areas. Simpson (1993, Chapter 2), following Uspensky (1973) and Fowler (1996 [1986]), distinguishes the following different kinds of viewpoint: (i) spatial viewpoint (the physical 'angle' of the viewpoint), (ii) temporal viewpoint, the time 'angle' (Chatman 1978 links (i) and (ii) together to form what he calls perceptual point of view), (iii) psychological viewpoint (what Chatman calls conceptual point of view), and (iv) ideological viewpoint (the socio-political slant put on what is described). To this list we would want to add a fifth kind of viewpoint, (v) social viewpoint (which expresses social relationships between the person whose viewpoint is being represented and other characters), and we would want to note that, within the notion of ideological viewpoint, room needs to be made to take account of personal attitudinal values (e.g. that a particular character 'is horrible' or 'looks kind'), as well as those associated with social or political groups. This kind of subdivision of the different elements involved in the concept of viewpoint usefully enables more descriptive precision. We would also want to be more precise about the linguistic features which are prototypically used to indicate viewpoint, in the spirit seen in Fowler (1996 [1986], Chapter 9) and Short (1996, Chapter 9), though we would also want to note that sometimes, as Short (1999) shows, viewpoint can be indicated by linguistic factors very different from the 'list of usual suspects'. Nonetheless, checklists of linguistic features prototypically associated with particular phenomena are useful in helping analysts and students to be more systematic (cf Short 1996 passim).
11.3.2 Narratological aspects of viewpoint
The distinction between discoursal and fictional point of view mentioned above, with its distinction between the point of view of the teller and that of some character being represented in the story told, brings us to a series of issues concerning discoursal viewpoint, which centres on Chapter 8 of Style in Fiction, 'Discourse and the discourse situation'.
The reason that twentieth-century criticism of the novel in particular has focused on the study of viewpoint so much is that, compared with prototypical poems and plays, there are more viewpoints to take into account in the discourse structure of the prototypical novel. The use of the level of
299
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
narration means that there is at least one more discourse level (with another addresser and addressee to take into account). We also think that our characterisation of the various ways in which the different discourse levels can 'collapse' together in particular novels, leading to different 'discourse architectures', is helpful in characterising discoursal viewpoint, although we would want to make it clear that 'discourse architectures' in novels are not necessarily unchanging, like the architecture of buildings. Indeed, in particular novels the discourse architecture may change quite a lot, and in either sudden or subtle ways, which, when charted, can lead to insights about viewpoint manipulation and the nature of the particular novel examined. A good example of this is the 582-word prologue at the beginning of Jane Gardam's Bilgewater (discussed in some detail in Short, Culpeper and Semino 2000). This prologue begins, for the first 135 words, with a third-person narration combined with the fictional viewpoint of a prospective undergraduate being interviewed for a place at a Cambridge college. Then, in sentence 14, the narration slips into second-person mode, and by sentence 33, 137 words later (which include 63 words of free direct speech and free direct thought), the narration has moved to the first-person and the tense of the narration has shifted from the past to the present (thus causing a possible ambiguity with free direct thought). Hence we begin with an apparently objective third-person narration, even though the narrator describes things from the prospective undergraduate's viewpoint, and are shifted in stages to a much more intimate and potentially limited and flawed viewpoint, a shift relatable to a tactic which leads to a surprise at the end of the novel. These shifts in discourse architecture within a relatively short passage, as Short, Culpeper and Semino (2000) point out, also raise an interesting theoretical issue about what it means to say that a particular (section of a) novel is written in a particular narrative form, and how we make these overall judgments about kinds of narration. For example, is it reasonable to say, as we have done above, that the prologue of Bilgewater slips into second-person narration for just a few words? And if it is not, how should we best describe the shift that takes place?
There are some things, however, that we would want to revisit and possibly change. We no longer share the conviction that the 'implied author'—'implied reader' level is really needed in the discourse structure of the novel (and indeed Short (1996) does not use it). It seems to have the status of a useful heuristic which may well not be theoretically necessary, and is potentially misleading in that, in spite of the term 'implied', it suggests a 'reified' addresser and addressee at that level, equivalent to an author, a narrator or a character (cf Nünning 1993). Although critics from Booth (1961) onwards have used the 'implied author/reader' concepts, it is interesting to note that those writing about non-literary writing (e.g. newspapers, letters) do not seem to feel the need for such concepts. Instead, they rely on the notion that when we communicate with one another we do not
300
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on
just instantiate our own viewpoints, but at the same time take into account the viewpoint(s) of those we are communicating with. When describing the narratorial aspect of discoursal viewpoint, we made the traditional critical distinction between first-person and third-person narration. In 1981, there were not many cases of second-person narration in novels. But recently the number has increased somewhat, and narratological research on them has become more extensive (see, for example, Fludernik 1996, Chapters 6 and 7). Now we would need to discuss second-person narrations as well. Interestingly, as the pronoun number ordering might lead one to expect, second-person narrations seem, to some degree at least, to occupy a position between first- and third-person narrations. Such narrations often begin with a third-person-like generic 'you' and then shift as the narration develops, into a second-person equivalent of '1-narration' (as in Half Asleep in Frog Pyjamas by Tom Robbins, for example). But the system of effects of particular forms of narration clearly needs to be reviewed, particularly when it is remembered that in addition to second-person narrations it is also now possible to find plural narration forms (involving 'we' or 'they': see Margolin 1996 and Fludernik 1996, pp. 223–249) and what Fludernik (1996, pp. 249–266) calls 'odd' narrative tenses.
In discussing point of view in Chapter 5 we made use of the notion of a 'reflector' for the 'person whose [fictional] point of view is represented' (see Section 5.5.1, p. 139). The term 'focaliser' has since become a much more popular term for this role. But, as indicated in Short (1999), some of us now have some reservations about using these terms. They tend to suggest that just one person's view is represented in a story, and it is true that one person's point of view often predominates in particular stories. But careful examination of viewpoint shifts in short extracts often shows that the reflector/focaliser may change from sentence to sentence or even within sentences. It is important that we do not unwarily allow terms like 'the focaliser' to prevent us from picking up on such changes and variations, and indeed the possibility of variability in viewpoint is of ever-present importance in fictional prose.
Similar remarks can be applied to the concept of mind style, to which we devoted Chapter 6 of our book. It is arguable that the notion of conceptual viewpoint (see section 11.3.1 and Chatman (1978)) effectively includes the notion of mind style, except that the conceptual viewpoint being expressed is abnormal in some way. And the borderline between mind style and ideological viewpoint is sometimes not easy to maintain. Of course, as with discussions of particular ‘implied authors’ or focalised viewpoints in particular texts, these remarks do not negate the value of particular discussions of mind styles in particular texts (e.g. Bockting 1995, Semino 2002, 2006, Semino and Swindlehurst 1996). The issue is a more theoretical one of asking, Occam-like, how many terms and concepts we need to describe to account for the phenomena we are examining.
301
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
11.3.3 Speech, thought and writing presentation
In 1981 Chapter 10 of Style in Fiction represented an advance on previous accounts of discourse presentation in the novel in a number of respects. It brought more analytical precision to the study of discourse presentation than before, and has since been used by a number of other analysts (e.g. Roeh and Nir 1990, Simpson 1993, Thompson 1996). It distinguished speech presentation and thought presentation systematically, by allocating them to separate, parallel scales, showed how the presentational scales connected to narration, and argued for a presentational cline along which the various categories, distinguished by a combination of linguistic and contextual means, were associated with varying degrees of faithfulness to an original. It argued for a placing of the free direct category at the extreme of the 'direct' end of the scale (previous accounts, if they made clear the placings of the various categories in relation to one another at all, tended to situate free direct speech in between direct speech and free indirect speech). It also argued for a new presentational category, the narrator's report of a speech act (and its thought presentation equivalent), to account for sentences like 'She demanded an apology for the tone of his request', which indicates the speech act value (and often the topic of the speech act, as here) of the utterance presented, but not its propositional content, as found in indirect speech. Finally, it tried to explain, via reference to different presentational norms, how the same presentational category could have different effects on the speech and thought presentation scales, with the FIS/FIT contrast being the starkest.
Since that time, research in the area of discourse presentation has continued with some vigour (see, for example, Fludernik 1993, a monumental work which exhaustively describes research up to that date and makes a number of influential theoretical claims) and a reincarnation of Style in Fiction now would need to take account of the relevant literature and the advances made. We would particularly want to take account of the findings of the Lancaster speech, writing and thought presentation research projects, which have taken the original Style in Fiction model and applied it systematically and exhaustively not only to a corpus of written fictional and non-fictional narratives, but to a corpus of spoken English, in order to see how well it stands up to rigorous application to a wide range of data. The work on the written corpus, which has been reported in Semino and Short (2004) and a series of articles by the research team they led, has shown that, by and large, the original Style in Fiction model works well when applied to non-fictional narratives as well as to the fictional narratives it was originally developed for. Among other things, the corpus work has enabled useful comparisons between fictional and non-fictional text types (see Short, Semino and Culpeper 1996 and Short, Semino and Wynne 1999). There is no space to go into much detail here, but the Lancaster corpus work has, among other things, established the need for
302
<PAGE>
Stylistics and fiction 25 years on

(i) another scale of presentation, in addition to those of speech and thought: writing presentation (which will be of particular relevance for the epistolary novel), and
(ii) another category on the speech presentation scale to account for sentences even more minimal than the narrator's report of a speech act category, as described above: narrator's representation of voice (nv).
The introduction of a narrator's representation of voice category, and its equivalents on the thought and writing presentation scales, to account for sentences where utterance is reported even more minimally (e.g. 'They talked quietly to one another'), effectively completes the range of 'faithfulness claims' that can be made when reporting someone else's speech (or writing):

1. Speech occurred.
2. Speech occurred + speech act (and topic) specified.
3. Speech occurred + speech act (and topic) and propositional content specified.
4. Speech occurred + speech act (and topic), propositional content and the words and structures used to utter that propositional content specified.
More detail can be found in Semino, Short and Culpeper (1997), Short, Semino and Wynne (2002) and Short (2003). Semino, Short and Wynne (1999) discuss how discourse presentation theory and the possible-worlds theory described briefly in section 11.2.2 can be interestingly combined to analyse hypothetical discourse (e.g. a character thinking about what he or she might say to someone else, but does not).
11.4 Detail and precision, and the way ahead
Much of what we have been describing in section 11.3 above instantiates the need to take into account the work of the last 25 years, which, building on the previous work of others, has helped us to understand better, and in more detail, particular aspects of the stylistic analysis of fictional (and non-fictional) texts. The areas we have picked out for particular comment, although they are major topics in the stylistics of fiction, are by no means the only areas we could have mentioned. For example, our brief encounter with speech act theory in Chapter 9 would now need to be adapted and expanded to take account of more recent work on indirect speech acts, deviant speech acts, speech act ambiguity and the way in which some speech acts take a number of conversational turns to achieve completion. Another area to which there would be much to add is that of 'textual rhetoric', the subject of Chapter 7: here we would need to take account of
303
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
recent decades of work on the mental processing of texts, leading to a fuller understanding of narrative from the perspective of reading and comprehension (Emmott 1997, Emmott, Sanford and Morrow 2006). There is plenty more to do in areas we already know quite a lot about, and there are bound to be more insights and new methods of analysis for these areas in the future.
And indeed, even if these 'old' areas were now completely understood (which they are clearly not), there are still aspects of stylistic involvement with novels that we have hardly begun to account for as yet. The stylistic explications of theme and evaluation are the most obvious areas awaiting development. And of course novelists keep on writing interesting new novels for us to analyse too. For those who found the first edition of Style in Fiction quite a strenuous read, the new version, if written from scratch, could scarcely avoid being much more so. In reflecting on this we bring the present chapter to a timely end.
304
<PAGE>
CHAPTER 12
'The Bucket and the Rope'
This final additional chapter of our second edition has the goal of filling in two significant gaps in our treatment of style and fiction up to this point. First, we need to illustrate more fully, through concrete textual examples, the newer developments in the stylistics of fiction introduced and explained in Chapter 11. Second, we feel it is important to attempt the stylistic analysis of a whole fictional text. It is a besetting weakness of the stylistics of fiction that it is partial, if not fragmentary: we find ourselves analysing a selection of linguistic features only, or analysing a small excerpt from a larger text. Attempting the analysis of a complete text is indeed essential if we are to illustrate properly the narratological theme introduced in the last chapter. Other approaches to fictional texts also gain from their integration within an overall treatment of a single work of fiction. We hope, therefore, that it will be seen as a fitting finale to the book to tackle here the analysis of a short story, using the techniques introduced in earlier chapters, and particularly the newer approaches introduced in Chapter 11.
How 'short' should such a short story be? Our answer is: very short indeed! For this purpose we have chosen 'The Bucket and the Rope', a story by T.F. Powys, first published in 1927 in a volume called Fables. It contains a mere 2739 words, plus its five-word title. To have chosen a longer story would have meant expanding this chapter to gigantic proportions – and, even so, it is long enough for most tastes. The brevity of the story means that we will be able to illustrate a range of cognitively-oriented approaches, and more importantly, show their interrelations with one another: narratology or 'story logic'; worlds structure (possible worlds, text worlds); point of view; and characterisation. At the same time, we will call on more traditional aspects of literary text exegesis (allusion, metaphor) as well as drawing on the techniques of textual analysis illustrated in earlier chapters, such as Chapter 3.
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
We said that the approaches we have taken from Chapter 11 are 'cognitively-oriented'. They do indeed illustrate the 'cognitive turn' of present-day stylistics (although we also look back for some structuralist models in the analysis of story). That is to say, these frameworks illustrate the nature of literary interpretation, conceiving it as a cognitive process of 'making sense', in the broadest sense, of a story and the way of telling it. Using the terms of Culpeper's (2001, p. 28) discussion of characterisation (cf section 11.2.3), this entails taking account of a combination of top-down and bottom-up interpretative processes. From the top down come cognitive 'givens' such as schemas, narrative frameworks or scripts, cognitive metaphors and prototype categories,\(^{1}\) and the assumptions or expectations associated with these. From the bottom up come the observable linguistic features of the text; and the great challenge is to establish the connection between these two directions of analysis. For this purpose, one key tool in our explanatory equipment is inference (cf section 4.3.1), another is foregrounding – the recognition of salience against a background of 'the normal' (see sections 2.3 and 4.6) – which in this chapter can be tentatively extended from linguistic foregrounding to more abstract types of foregrounding, such as an unexpected turn in a narrative. We will make use of terms such as 'prototype', 'schema' and 'inference' (together with allied verbs and adjectives 'prototypical', 'schematic', 'inferencing', and so on). However, for reasons of space we often do not explain these cognitive processes and phenomena in full detail. The terms sometimes have to be regarded as shorthand tokens for a fuller explanation. Even with a very short story, a great deal of detail will have to be omitted or taken for granted.
Further, one approach introduced in Chapter 11 will unfortunately not be sufficiently exemplified in this chapter: the 'corpus linguistic turn' which we have placed alongside the 'cognitive turn' as one of the most significant recent research developments in stylistics. Choosing a very short story may be helpful for other kinds of stylistic analysis, but for corpus-based methods, the shorter the story, the less suitable it is. Corpus linguistic analysis thrives on large corpora such as the works of Henry James, where quantitative techniques can be reliably employed. Nevertheless, we will be able to make use of some insights and observations which come to the fore in corpus linguistics (e.g. collocational analysis, comparisons of frequency).
12.1 T.F. Powys
The author of ‘The Bucket and the Rope’, Theodore Francis Powys, could be unfamiliar to many readers and so some introductory information may be helpful. He was a brother of the better-known John Cowper Powys, the
306
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
author of The Glastonbury Romance, an extract from which was examined in section 6.3. T.F. Powys was a prolific essayist, novelist and short-story writer, and 'The Bucket and the Rope' (first published in 1927) was included in a posthumous anthology of Powys's stories called God's Eyes A-Twinkle (1947) and also in The Second Penguin Book of English Short Stories, edited by Christopher Dolley (1973). Powys's stories are usually about simple country folk from an area not unlike Dorset, where he lived for much of his life. The stories are idiosyncratic in that they invoke a world where people can talk to the dead by visiting their graves, where God or Christ can arrive in a village and talk to the inhabitants, and where non-human animals and objects (the bucket and the rope in this case) can converse and show human-like intelligence. Thematically, the stories dwell on the basic human predicament, and often on the twin themes of love and death. As Charles Prentice puts it in the preface to God's Eyes A-Twinkle:
Of all Powys's stories the Fables are farthest from everyday reality. Their conception leaves him free to depict the insignificance of man amid the huge disregarded company in which he lives – from seemingly dumb familiar objects to the hidden forces behind the mundane screen. Clearly, Good and Evil exist, though they change into each other bewilderingly; the good and honest life is good and honest; and humility and resignation are necessary to it. But no morals are drawn. Powys is no more a moralist than a philosopher – whatever moral and philosophical notions he may keep to himself.
[God's Eyes A-Twinkle, p. xiii]
In the context of these remarks about man's insignificance it is interesting to note that the second title for the collection from which 'The Bucket and the Rope' came, No Painted Plumage, appears to be an allusion to Thomas Gray's 'Ode on the Spring', in which the insignificant fly is described, in contrast to birds and more colourful insects, as having 'no painted plumage'. This, incidentally, was an allusion which we did not recognise at first, but which we found by typing the phrase into the Google search engine.
12.2 'The Bucket and the Rope'
We now present the story, so that our readers can familiarise themselves with it and reach their own conclusions, before we discuss it. We have numbered the paragraphs of the story so that we can refer to them more easily in later discussion. We have not numbered each sentence, as that would be more distracting for our readers, and in any case, most of the paragraphs are short.
307
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
The Bucket and the Rope
A bucket once lay upon its side in a little shed, that was a short way down a by-lane, near to the village of Shelton. [1]
This bucket, a large one, had been kicked over by a man who had hanged himself up by the neck, by means of an odd piece of rope, that he had tied to a strong beam. [2]
The man's name, who had hanged himself was Mr Dendy, who rented a few pleasant fields that he used to plough happily, and, besides keeping a few good cows, he fattened some nice pigs. [3]
Every servant, be he never so humble, is interested in his master, whose habits of life, goings and comings, loves and hates, are watched and commented upon. Mr Dendy's movements as well as his behaviour had always been of great interest to the bucket and the rope; who, when together, which was often the case, for they lived in the same shed, would speak of all that Mr Dendy did, and endeavour to find out as best they might a reason for his actions. [4]
Both were interested in any kind of life that was not like themselves, such as mankind, because both were humble and did not consider, as so many do, that they or their own kind deserved the most notice. [5]
In order to study men, both the bucket and the rope decided to take Mr Dendy as an example of humanity, believing, as they well might, that the ways and notions of a simple countryman ought to be easier to understand than those of one more sly and cunning. They wished to study Mr Dendy in order to find out from his behaviour what other men were like; to learn from his doings how they did, to find out the causes of their sorrows and joys, so as to journey a little nearer to the Truth that is always so hard to discover. [6]
Now and again the two friends had been a little puzzled by Mr Dendy, who did not often act as they would have expected him to, for sometimes he would seem to be troubled, when, according to the bucket's ideas of cause and effect, there was no reason for him to be so. [7]
And now that Mr Dendy had hanged himself, pressing both of them into this last service, to forward his self-destruction, the bucket and the rope thought they would review the man's life, in the hope of finding one true reason at least for his final act. [8]
'Is it not a little curious if not altogether surprising,' observed the bucket, 'that we should have been put to so sad a use in helping our good master die? Perhaps you can remember as well as I the joyful day when we were first purchased, which happened to be the very day before Mr Dendy was married. [9]
"He married as you know, a woman, a creature created to ease a man of the heavy burden of his desire, a burden as troublesome to carry as a kicking ass.' [10]
308
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
'And who also,' observed the rope, 'was intended to cook and prepare a man's food, to rear his children and to clean his house.' [11]
'That should certainly be so,' said the other. [12]
'The day we were purchased,' continued the rope, 'happened to be one of those delightful May days when all things animate and inanimate, that exist under the sun, are entirely happy. [13]
'I was coiled up in the shop window of Mr Johnson, the ropemaker, a man whose shirt-sleeves were always turned up, so that his hairy arms made the children stare. The sun shone upon me, and in its pleasant warmth I soon fell asleep. I dreamed of my happy childhood, when I grew up in a large field, beside a million brothers and sisters who were all beautiful flowers. But I did not sleep for long, for as soon as the sun rose too high to shine into the window I awoke and looked out into the street. [14]
‘Anyone with a proper desire for knowledge, if he has eyes, can always see something of interest in what goes on in a street. He has only to look and something will be sure to come near. [15]
'I began to watch the folk who moved along the pavement in front of the shop, and a few of them particularly attracted my notice. Two old women came by, whose feet seemed to stick to the stones at every step, while their tongues cackled and gabbled about the ill-conduct of their neighbours. [16]
'A grand military gentleman sauntered past, who saw his own reflection in every window that he went by, and became prouder than ever. A lady who followed him at a little distance wished to see herself too, but did not dare to look, because she feared that a servant girl who walked behind might notice what she did. [17]
'Presently there was a fine clatter of running feet; some schoolboys came by, pulling the caps from one another's heads, and then an alderman passed, who looked about him as if the town were all his own. [18]
'After him came two young and pleasing girls, who were ready for love; they watched coyly every young man in the street, and laughed in order to show what they longed for. [19]
'The clock in the church tower at the top of the town struck three, but no one seemed to give any heed to it, except a poor debtor, whose examination was to be at that very hour in the town hall, and who wished he had taken his wife's advice earlier, and drowned himself. [20]
'The clock had hardly finished striking when a young man, who had the joyful looks of a would-be bridegroom, together with a young girl entered the shop. I looked at her with admiration, and at him with pleasure. They seemed made for one another. Anyone could see that she had the sweetest of natures, that would be unlikely, for the fear of being cruel, to refuse anything a man might ask of her. The man was Mr Dendy, and she was to be his wife.' [21]
309
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
'Her arms had been opened to another before him,' murmured the bucket. [22]
'Only a grave could have prevented that' answered the rope; 'but allow me to continue: [23]
'Mr Dendy came forward to the window and looked at me, together with Mr Johnson. The girl looked elsewhere. Mr Johnson's hands, that were as hairy as his arms, took me up, uncoiled me, and stretched me out. Our master examined me for a little, satisfied that I was what he needed, and made the purchase. [24]
'Mr Dendy was about twenty-nine years old then, and the young girl about eighteen,' remarked the bucket. [25]
'So she was' said the rope, 'but it is curious to think now what she did next. While Mr Johnson and Mr Dendy were talking, she coiled and uncoiled me, and then, in her girlish amusement, for she looked at him lovingly, she made a running noose of me, slipped it over the master's head, and pulled it tight.' [26]
'Mr Johnson laughed, I suppose?' [27]
'Yes, but a little uneasily. While she toyed with me,' said the rope, 'I had a chance to look at her the more narrowly. She seemed just the creature to delight any man with her sweetness and eagerness for love. She had a yielding kindness, but no wickedness in her. She showed her good nature to a young man, the son of a lawyer, who happened to pass her in a by-street when Mr Dendy slipped into a small inn. The lawyer's son looked unhappy and she allowed him to kiss her, while Mr Dendy was out of the way.' [28]
