'She had a little foot,' observed the bucket, 'and a winning gait, and had Mr Dendy peeped through the dingy bar-window, when he was having a merry jest with Farmer Pardy, he should have been glad to see that the lawyer's son thought her as nice as he did.' [29]
'A rope would have fancied so,' said the other dryly. [30]
'Mr Dendy had no sooner bought you,' said the bucket, 'than he went to the ironmonger's and purchased me. We were carried off together, and so we became acquainted, and that very evening I was made use of to collect the swill for the pigs; I remember even now the unpleasant smell of those rotten potatoes.' [31]
'It was not the stink of the sour garbage that made our master hang himself,' observed the rope thoughtfully, 'for he would be often whistling when he brought you in, full of the nastiest stuff. Neither could it have been the weight of you that troubled him, for he would ever carry you jauntily, as if the burden of a few gallons of swill was nothing to so powerful an arm as his.' [32]
'Oh no, he never minded carrying me,' said the bucket, 'for whatever the time of year was, whether the summer sun shone, or whether the dreary autumn rain fell, Mr Dendy would bear me along with the same
310
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
sprightliness. He would perhaps tarry at a cottage gate, and have a merry word with the occupants, telling a droll story of country matters for the young girls to smile at, and bidding them to ask of his kind Betty what the fancies were that she had found the most useful in the getting of a husband.' [33]
'We could watch nearly all that he did,' remarked the rope, 'and he certainly appeared to be living a very happy life: the sweet country air, the plain and wholesome food that he ate, as well as his constant though not too tedious toil, gave him health and joy, and he was never in want of a shilling to spend when he needed one.' [34]
'Only once,' observed the bucket sadly, 'did I notice Mr Dendy act in a way that was not usual for a village man. He was bearing me, full, along a path from a small cottage where he bought swill. On each side of the path there were flowers, both white and yellow. Mr Dendy set me down, a rotten orange bobbed up on my surface. Mr Dendy rested by the path, plucked some of the flowers, and seemed to take delight in holding them in his hand.' [35]
'What did he do next?' asked the rope. [36]
'He carried the flowers home to his wife,' replied the bucket. . . . [37]
'The summer pleased Mr Dendy, and so did the winter,' said the rope. [38]
'In the winter we saw more of him, for we were used the more. During the winter the horses lay in, and straw had to be carried to them, and in the winter there were more pigs to be fattened. In the winter, too, a strong man feels his strength and his happiness more than in the summer. He learns to brave the keenest wind without a shudder, and cares nothing when the rain soaks him to the skin. No weather daunted Mr Dendy, and the more he bore with the storms outside the pleasanter was his parlour, with its cheerful light, and the warm presence of a wife who loved him.' [39]
'Why, then, did he hang himself?' asked the bucket. [40]
'The winter weather was certainly not to blame,' answered the rope, 'for I cannot think of those happy days without being sure that he enjoyed them. I was stouter then, and yet I think not, for I appear to be strong enough now to hold a pretty fair burden. Mr Dendy, who is carried by me, could carry a bundle then, he thought nothing of carrying as much straw with me as was enough for three men to bear. However large the bundle was, he would somehow get it upon his back, so that the straw upon either side of him would sweep the hedges in the lane, almost as though a whole stack was out a-walking.' [41]
'Yes, there was Mr Dendy!' exclaimed the bucket, 'a true and joyful countryman, doing his proper tasks. What could harm him? What could prevent him from living out his life contentedly and going down, as a
311
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
good man should, gently into the grave? Surely never was a poor man created who meant so well.' [42]
'Look at him now,' said the rope quietly; 'at first when he kicked you over I wondered if I should be strong enough to hold him. He struggled horribly, and I fancy that when he first felt me tighten round his throat he would have changed his mind. He tried to catch me to lessen the dreadful feeling of suffocation'. [43]
'You must have hurt him very much,' observed the bucket, 'for his face became quite black, and his eyes bulged out of his head. I wonder you did not let him fall, for in his death agony he kicked his legs and swung round, but you held him fast. Why did he do it?' [44]
'I believe the reason was,' replied the rope, 'that Mr Dendy did not like to see others happy.' [45]
'This is not easy to believe,' remarked the bucket, 'when one considers how happy he was himself.' [46]
'His wife made him so,' said the rope 'and feeling her success with him she naturally wished to make another happy too.' [47]
'What could be more proper?' said the bucket. [48]
'It was this summer,' continued the rope, 'and the master, having saved a few guineas, bought for himself a new Sunday suit "You look so well in it," his wife told him, "that you should go to church more often, for people will know how well you are getting on when they see you in your new clothes." Now, it was the time when Mr Dendy began to go to church of an evening that I noticed passing this shed the same young man who had given Betty a kiss in the by-street when Mr Dendy was drinking a glass in a little tavern. He still looked unhappy.' [49]
'A chance for Betty to turn his sorrow into joy!' laughed the bucket. [50]
'She wished to do so, and they met in this very shed, on a Sunday evening, when Mr Dendy was supposed to be gone to church.' [51]
'But had he gone?' asked the bucket. [52]
'No,' replied the rope. 'He had only put on the best clothes and walked out, as if to go. Instead of going to church, he came to this shed, took me up, and bound me round in a large bundle of straw. The bundle he placed against the wall of the shed, where there was a little chink, and, creeping under the straw to hide himself, he waited.' [53]
'For the pleasure of witnessing the kindness of his wife, I suppose,' said the bucket. [54]
'One would have thought so,' replied the rope, 'but the look upon Mr Dendy's face when he saw what was going on did not warrant such a supposition.' [55]
'Perhaps he thought,' reasoned the bucket, 'that Betty should have remained at home and warmed the rabbit pie for his supper; for the sermon preached by Mr Hayball always made him extremely hungry,
312
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
and Betty was not to be expected to know that he was not at church. I have seen the pigs fed so often, and I know how hungry animals are, and, as food keeps man alive and prevents him from becoming a dead carcass, it is natural that a man should wish the woman that he keeps to prepare for him, even though she may prefer to be loving and kind to another man.' [56]
'You should have heard Mr Dendy,' said the rope; 'he gnashed his teeth and moaned horribly, and when his wife's friend seemed to be altogether forgetting his sorrow, being come, as the lyrical poet says, "Where comfort is --" Mr Dendy crept out of the bundle and hid in the lane, snarling like a bitten dog.' [57]
'His hunger, I suppose, had gone beyond the proper bounds,' suggested the bucket. [58]
'It is difficult' said the rope, after a few minutes' silence, as the body swung to and fro, 'for us to decide what could have troubled this good man. No one had robbed him. No one had beaten him or hurt him, and never once since they had been married had Betty refused his embraces.' [59]
'It must have been that nosegay,' exclaimed the bucket. [60]
12.3 Discussion of 'The Bucket and the Rope'
12.3.1 Provisional interpretative comments on the story
Before examining different aspects of the story and applying various techniques to them, we begin with a brief interpretation of what we think this rather enigmatic story is about.
In our view, the story is thematically concerned with life and death, positive and negative, and appearance v. reality. In it, the bucket and the rope try to work out why their owner, Mr Dendy, committed suicide (a negative factor) in spite of his being a naturally good and happy man (a positive factor). Mr Dendy used the bucket and the rope to kill himself, so they are directly involved, as well as interested students of humankind. But their schematic assumptions about how the world works, and should work, are different from ours. Although we are not told explicitly, we infer that Mr Dendy kills himself because he loves his wife and cannot bear it when he discovers her making love to the lawyer's son. But the bucket and the rope cannot see this. They are utilitarians, and so when they see Mr Dendy's wife satisfying both her husband and the lawyer's son sexually, they see this not as a problem, but as a good thing. However, the story is not just a comparison of two different visions of the same story. It also seems to suggest that although the bucket and the rope get it wrong, in mistaking the motives and feelings involved in the case, in another sense they get it right;
313
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
their attitude seems more 'logical' than that of Mr Dendy (and most readers of the story). In other words, the story 'deconstructs' or 'problem- atises' our standard assumptions about human love and sexual relations.
Another remarkable thing about the story relates to viewpoint and our attitude to what is described. It is about someone who has just committed suicide, but, because of the rather enigmatic and offhand way in which things are presented to us, instead of becoming involved emotionally with the man who killed himself, we are prompted to consider what happened in a more dispassionate way (which in turn helps us more easily to 'problematise' our own assumptions which the story draws on).
12.3.2 The title of the story: schemata and associations
A story's title can legitimately be considered part of the text, and part of what has to be interpreted. While this chapter was being written, a Google internet search for the phrase 'the bucket and the rope' resulted in 202 'hits', of which, after those for the title of Powys's story are removed, almost all are related in some way to wells and the schema of drawing water from wells. Some of the texts in which the phrase occurs are mathematical puzzles but the majority are stories, many of which are religious or related to mythology. The well, with its life-giving water, recurs most frequently in Christian and Muslim contexts in the Google set. This suggests that, for many, the phrase will be associated with life and possibly have religious associations as well. However, if we search for 'bucket' in the British National Corpus (BNC) we find an association between 'bucket' and the verb 'kick', as in 'kick the bucket', which is an colloquial idiom for dying. A Google search for 'kick the bucket' reveals 277,000 hits. We examined the first 50, of which 72 per cent were dictionary definitions of the phrase or straightforward 'die' uses. \( ^{2} \)  In addition, the phrase 'the rope' has a strong association with death by hanging. Hence the title may well be perceived by readers as prefiguring the life/death theme, which is, of course, appropriate, given that the bucket and the rope are trying to work out why Mr Dendy has committed suicide, which he did by literally 'kicking the bucket'.
The other notable schematic connection for the title is with the fable genre. We have noted that 'The Bucket and the Rope' comes from a collection originally called Fables, and we may also recognise that the noun phrase structure 'the X and the Y' (as in 'the Hare and the Tortoise') is a common type of title for stories in Aesop's famous fables, with X and Y referring to animals or inanimate objects, and indeed this is also the case for the other stories in the Fables/No Painted Plumage collection. In many of Aesop's fables, as here, we meet animals and inanimate objects which talk, and thus enter into what Ryan (1991) calls a textual actual world (TAW) resembling our world in many ways (and so we can take many of our actual world
314
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
assumptions along to it) but with an expanded inventory of characters. The title of the story, as well as the volume it first appeared in, thus sets us up to infer a 'moral' for the story, even if, in line with Prentice's comments in section 12.1, Powys does not tell us what the moral should be.
The last thing to note about the title is that it suggests that the story is about the bucket and the rope, in spite of the fact that we are likely to want to see it as being about the human beings in the story, and Mr Dendy in particular, because of our tendency to search for 'human significance' in texts (see Fairley 1988). We will return to these issues raised by the title below.
12.3.3 The story's discourse structure: narration, speech presentation and 'framing'
'The Bucket and the Rope' is a third-person narration in which the first eight paragraphs (420 words, or 15.3 per cent of the story) are entirely narration and the rest of the story is direct speech produced by the bucket and the rope, with no narrative intervention apart from the associated reporting clauses. These reporting clauses comprise only a further 132 words. If these are added to the first eight paragraphs of narration, we can see that only about 20 per cent of the story is narration and about 80 per cent of it is direct speech. At first sight, this helps to give the story a straightforward and simple air: the third-person narration (cf section 8.1.2b) is canonically associated with objective presentation of the 'facts of the story', and the direct speech mode creates the illusion that the narrator/author is 'interfering' with the speech presented (cf Figure 10.1 in section 10.1.3) in only minimal ways.
An examination of the relationship between the reporting and reported clauses confirms this straightforward and simple 'feel'. The conversation between the bucket and the rope has 41 direct speech turns, and all but two of these turns have an accompanying reporting clause, making it easy to attribute each turn to the relevant character. The reporting clauses themselves are also very straightforward. Only five of them contain anything other than a simple VS grammatical structure containing a verb followed by a simple determiner + noun reference to the relevant speaker (e.g. 'replied the rope'). Of these five, four contain a one-word manner adverb and one (the rope's last turn in the story) contains a twelve-word time reference: 'said the rope, after a few minutes' silence, as the body swung to and fro'. This clause, with its associated turn, is therefore foregrounded by its length as well as being prominent as the penultimate turn in the story. It seems to summarise the reasons for the bucket's and the rope's perplexity and brings us back to the realisation that the bucket and the rope are conducting their conversation across the body of the man who has used them to kill himself.
315
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
The story's rather clear division between the third-person narration and the dialogue between the bucket and the rope marks a distinction between the two main parts of the narrative: the 'framing' initial narration followed by the 'framed' direct speech dialogue. In the initial narration we are told that, in chronological terms, immediately before the beginning of the presentation of the story, the bucket and the rope have been used by Mr Dendy to commit suicide, and the bucket then suggests (in paragraph [9]) that this act is 'curious, if not altogether surprising'. Indeed, the narrator tells us that the bucket and the rope, who decide to review Mr Dendy's life in order to understand humankind better, are 'a little puzzled' [7] by a number of other things he has done in the past. The direct speech then contains descriptions of what the bucket and the rope saw, and their attempts to find a plausible (for them) hypothesis to explain Mr Dendy's suicide. The third-person past-tense narrative frame thus describes what is the fictional present and immediate past for the bucket and the rope, and in the direct speech dialogue (which also exists in their fictional present) they recall past occurrences they found to be significant and, later in the story (from [32] onwards), they compare suggestions on why Mr Dendy killed himself. Unlike most framing narratives, however, there is no real return to the narrative frame at the end of the story, apart from the final and apparently climactic minimal reporting clause, 'exclaimed the bucket' on which the story ends. In deictic shift theory terms (cf Galbraith (1995) and section 12.3.7 below), there is little opportunity for us to 'pop' back up to the deictic centre with which the story opens).
12.3.4 The story's 'structure'
The above description suggests that an account of the story/plot structure of 'The Bucket and the Rope' will need two parts, one related to the framing story and one to the framed story. Drawing on various narratological frameworks, we will outline and combine a set of different 'story structure' accounts below, but first, a few words of warning concerning analyses of story 'structure'. As reported in section 11.2.1, Herman (2002), among others, has shown that what he calls 'story logic' is not straightforwardly observable. Different readers may come up with alternative accounts, depending on the inferences that they make as they read the text, and there is also a question of how much specification of detail (see section 5.3) is found in a particular account of a story. In this respect, stories can be like textual summaries, which can be more or less detailed, depending on their interpretative purposes. Indeed, it is likely that literary analysts choose a 'structure' and level of detail when outlining what they see as the 'story logic' of a particular text so that they can relate it optimally to the interpretative account they are giving. What we provide below is merely an account which we find interpretatively plausible and for which we would
316
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
want to argue on the basis of a range of evidence. There is, moreover, an interesting empirical question of whether different people's specifications of the structure of the story will be substantially different, leading to different interpretative consequences, or slightly different instantiations of the 'same' structure, rather like notational variants in formal accounts of grammar.
A fable is normally a story told chronologically, whereas this story is not. It will help, then, if we summarise the events of the 'The Bucket and the Rope' in chronological order (the fabula in Russian formalist terms), as far as we can discern it, in preparation for further analysis:

1. The day before his wedding, Mr Dendy buys the bucket and the rope to use on his smallholding.
2. Betty, the prospective Mrs Dendy, who is with Mr Dendy when he buys the rope, appears to be attracted to, and involved with, the lawyer's son when she is betrothed to Mr Dendy (who is eleven years older than her).
3. The bucket and the rope observe Mr Dendy over some years as he uses them. He appears to be 'a true and joyful countryman, doing his proper tasks', and from their perspective, the only thing that he does out of the ordinary is to pick a bunch of flowers which he gives to his wife.
4. Mr Dendy observes his wife and the lawyer's son making love in his barn while they think he is at church.
5. Mr Dendy kills himself, hanging himself by means of the bucket and the rope.
6. The bucket and the rope try to work out why Mr Dendy killed himself.
7. The bucket naively concludes that the bunch of flowers was the cause of Mr Dendy's suicide.
To begin our analysis, we will employ the structuralist account of Claude Brémond, allied with the possible worlds approach of Marie-Laure Ryan and Lévi-Strauss's interpretative structuralist analysis. The structuralist accounts of 'story logic' will need to be backed up by inferential analysis of the sort suggested by Herman (2002), as reported in section 11.2.1.
12.3.5 Structuralist and possible worlds accounts of literary narratives: Claude Brémond and Marie-Laure Ryan
Brémond (1973) suggests that stories go through one or more narrative cycles, from a state of narrative equilibrium through a process of mediation to a state of disequilibrium, and then, perhaps, back again, via a 'mediation' process to a state of equilibrium again, and so on (see also Figure 12.2 on p. 319):
317
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
![](images/page_10_0.jpg)

Figure 12.1
If we apply this schema for the 'what happened' part of a story to the above summary of 'The Bucket and the Rope', we can perceive two interconnected, but distinct, narrative cycles, depending on the viewpoint from which the events are perceived.
The framing narrative begins, from the bucket and the rope's point of view, with episode 5 in the list above, which is a disequilibrium (they are puzzled by Mr Dendy's suicide). The mediation takes place in 6, in their attempt to understand why he did it, and this attempt includes their recall of the earlier events described in 1–4. This re-ordering of the chronological order of the events (the sjuizhet in Russian formalist terms) helps us to understand how the story is presented in an exceptional sequence. In presentational order, the episodes of the story are as follows:

5. Mr Dendy kills himself, hanging himself by means of the bucket and the rope.
6. The bucket and the rope try to work out why Mr Dendy killed himself.
1. The day before his wedding, Mr Dendy bought the bucket and the rope to use on his smallholding.
2. Betty, the prospective Mrs Dendy, who is with Mr Dendy when he buys the rope, appears to be attracted to, and involved with,the lawyer's son when she is betrothed to Mr Dendy (who is eleven years older than her).
3. The bucket and the rope observe Mr Dendy over some years as he uses them. He appears to be 'a true and joyful countryman, doing him as projectors', and from their perspective, the only thing that he does out of the ordinary is to pick a bunch of flowers which he gives to his wife.
4. Mr Dendy observes his wife and the lawyer's son making love in his barn while they think he is at church.
7. The bucket naively concludes that the bunch of flowers was the cause of Mr Dendy's suicide.
The final statement in both the chronological and presentational ordering is 7, an apparent state of equilibrium for the bucket, who makes the final remark. Given that the story ends at this point and we are not told that the rope dissents from this conclusion, we can assume for the present (but see section 12.3.7) that the rope's viewpoint is the same as the bucket's.
However, looking at the story from Mr Dendy's angle, the movement is in the opposite direction: from equilibrium to disequilibrium – that is,
318
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
from the happy life he enjoys to the fact that he lives with a wife who is unfaithful to him, which he can 'resolve' only through suicide:
The bucket and the rope's narrative cycle
Disequilibrium → Mediation
↓
Equilibrium
Mr Dendy's narrative cycle
Equilibrium → Mediation
↓
Disequilibrium
Figure 12.2
If we apply Ryan's possible-worlds theory to the bucket and the rope's narrative cycle, we can see how her account of possible worlds helps to explain what drives the story forward. In her terms the initial disequilibrium in the bucket and the rope's narrative cycle relate to the fact that they know that their private knowledge world (K-world) conflicts with the textual actual world (TAW): they know that they do not know why Mr Dendy committed suicide. This is made clear in the last two pure narrative paragraphs [7] and [8], and also in the first part of the bucket's succeeding utterance, which opens the conversation between the bucket and the rope:
Now and again the two friends had been a little puzzled by Mr Dendy, who did not often act as they would have expected him to, for sometimes he would seem to be troubled, when, according to the bucket's ideas of cause and effect, there was no reason for him to be so. [7]
And now that Mr Dendy had hanged himself, pressing both of them into this last service, to forward his self-destruction, the bucket and the rope thought they would review the man's life, in the hope of finding one true reason at least for his final act. [8]
'Is it not a little curious if not altogether surprising,' observed the bucket, 'that we should have been put to so sad a use in helping our good master die? [...]' [9]
This lack of fit between the K-world and the TAW is indicated in the narrative by the italicised parts of the passages above, especially words like puzzled, surprising, curious. In terms of foregrounding, Mr Dendy's actions are deviant and hence noticeable by the standards of the bucket and the rope, whereas our own schemas and cause-and-effect relations are presumably much more in tune with Mr Dendy's. This TAW/K-world disequilibrium induces what Ryan calls a wish world (W-world) for the bucket and the rope: they want to make the TAW and their K-world consistent with one another. Their desire for this W-world, with the help of pragmatic inferencing via Grice's (1975) maxim of relation or Sperber and
319
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
Wilson's (1986) principle of relevance, pushes the conversation between the bucket and the rope forward, as they propose and reject in turn a series of possible explanations for Mr Dendy's behaviour:

(a) It was not the stink of the sour garbage [32].
(b) It was not the weight of the bucket [32], [33].
(c) It was not his life: he led a happy life, lived in 'the sweet country air', enjoyed his work and did not want for money [34].
(d) He picked a bunch of flowers and gave them to his wife [35]–[37] which the bucket recorded as 'not usual for a village man' (in this sense a foregrounded occurrence, but not taken up by the bucket or the rope as a reason for Mr Dendy's behaviour).
(e) It was not the weather [39], [41].
(f) He did not like to 'see others happy' [45] (the bucket rejects this suggestion by the rope).
(g) It was not his wife: she made him happy [47].
(h) Betty should have stayed at home to cook his dinner rather than make the lawyer's son happy [56] (this suggestion is apparently rejected as it is not taken up).
(i) 'His hunger, I suppose, had gone beyond the proper bounds [...]' [58] (again, this suggestion is apparently rejected as it is not taken up).
(j) 'It must have been that nosegay' [60] (this return to (d) appears to 'win out' as it is the suggestion which ends the story, but see section 12.3.9).
If we now use the Brémond narrative cycle to represent the framed story from Mr Dendy's point of view, rather than that of the bucket and the rope, note that (i) we are told of Mr Dendy's suicide as early as the narrative paragraph [3], (ii) we are given the rest of 'his' narrative cycle through the conversation of the bucket and the rope, and (iii) in order to see the elements of the cycle we have to infer them, because they are not explicitly stated by the bucket and the rope and their schemata for cause and effect is not the same as Mr Dendy's and ours would be. Figure 12.2 translates into Table 12.1 as follows.
Table 12.1
<table><tr><td>1. Disequilibrium</td><td>(a) Mr Dendy loves his wife but (b) she loves the lawyer&#x27;s son.</td></tr><tr><td>2. Mediation</td><td>Mr Dendy wants (a) but not (b).</td></tr><tr><td>3. Equilibrium</td><td>He kills himself (thus resolving the ‘paradox’ by removing the first half of the opposition).</td></tr></table>
320
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
This particular narrative cycle is not straightforward to arrive at because (i) there are alternatives for describing the initial disequilibrium stage in particular, and (ii) it is not always possible to find statements in the text which state the propositions in the cycle as presented above, partly because we see the disequilibrium and mediation phases through the words of the bucket and the rope, who do not properly understand what is going on. As a consequence, we have to rely rather more on inferences based on our schematic assumptions about social life. Effectively, we are 'filling in' Brémond's rather abstract narrative cycle schema with particular content.
We are told that Mr Dendy married Betty in [9] and [10], but nowhere in the story is there a proposition which states that Mr Dendy loved her. Hence this needs to be inferred by the reader and argued for by the analyst. Schematically it is likely that Mr Dendy loved his wife, given that she was young (eleven years younger than him) and attractive, and that he chose to marry her. This assumption is later strengthened by the fact that the bucket presupposes that Betty loved Mr Dendy (in a relative clause at the end of [39]) and that in [59] the rope states that 'never once since they had been married had Betty refused his embraces'. The only thing which could count as a clear sign of Mr Dendy's love for his wife is the fact that he picks the bunch of flowers and gives them to her.
There are also issues in relation to the second of the coordinated propositions in the disequilibrium line above. He wave used the verb 'love' partly to bring out lexically the opposition between the two propositions, and it is implicated that Betty loved the lawyer's son when she married Mr Dendy (cf [22] and [28]). But the opposition would still be there in schematic terms if 'loves his wife' in the first proposition was replaced by 'is married to Betty' and/or if 'loves' in the second proposition was replaced by 'has sex with'. This is because in our society (indeed most societies) love, marriage and sexual relations are very closely intertwined schematically.
The bucket's presupposition that Betty was 'loving and kind' to the lawyer's son just before Mr Dendy committed suicide [56] can easily be understood as a reference to sexual relations between them. The rope implicates strongly that Mr Dendy witnessed his wife in flagrante delicto with the lawyer's son [55], [57]. This implicated reference to the sexual act is foregrounded and strengthened by an allusion to a poem by Thomas Campion called 'Amaryllis':
[...] when his wife's friend seemed to be altogether forgetting his sorrow, being come, as the lyrical poet says, 'Where comfort is -' Mr Dendy crept out of the bundle and hid in the lane, snarling like a bitten dog. [57]
In Campion's poem I care not for these ladies (which we found using a Google search on 'come where comfort is'), Amaryllis is a country girl who is free with her favours compared with women who come from the town
321
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
or higher social orders, and the refrain at the end of each of the three verses of the poem is 'But when we come where comfort is, she never will say no.' The response of Mr Dendy to what he sees seems schematically to confirm the sexual act interpretation.
The mediation proposition 2 above 'Mr Dendy wants (a) but not (b)' also cannot be found in the text, as the bucket and the rope do not see any problem in Betty giving her sexual favours both to her husband and the lawyer's son (indeed they see her satisfying both men as an advantage - cf [21], [28], [29], [47] and [54]). Again, schematic assumptions provide the otherwise missing link between the text and proposition 2: those who are married to (or are in love with or involved in long-term sexual relations with) another are disposed to want those relations to be exclusive for their partners.
What is the explanation for 'he kills himself' being the equilibrium line? There are two parts to this: (a) this proposition needs to be systematically relatable to the disequilibrium line which began the narrative cycle, and (b) as one of the two coordinated propositions in the disequilibrium line is removed, the application of logic means that the paradox is also removed.
In the next section we will try to be more explicit about the relationship between structure and understanding by relating it to Claude Lévi-Strauss's anthropological analyses of the structure of myths.
12.3.6 Linking structure and interpretation: Claude Lévi-Strauss
Lévi-Strauss argued in a series of publications (e.g. 1963, 1964, 1970) that the story structure of myths can be rearranged from the syntagmatic axis (the chronological order of the events of the story) into a paradigmatic set of four columns (A, B, C, D) in which the events can be seen to be related to one another through two thematic oppositions (A v. B and C v. D). He then suggests that myths can be understood as constituting narrative 'mediations' of sets of paradoxical beliefs which societies have, through the oppositions in the four paradigmatic columns. We do not have the space to go more fully into Lévi-Strauss's analyses here, but we do think that myths and fables are not far apart, and that it is insightful to resolve the events of stories into sets of binary oppositions. Many critics talk of particular literary texts as instantiating one or more pairs of thematic oppositions (e.g. life/death, nature/culture, appearance/reality), and Hendricks (1973b) has interestingly applied the Lévi-Straussian approach to a story by Ambrose Bierce, using a two-column thematic opposition, an approach we adopt below. It is proposed, then, that overall textual interpretation relates to the specific working out of one or more pairs of thematic oppositions, which is what we now attempt.
In Table 12.2, we take the two Brémond-style narrative cycles which we have argued for above, and juxtapose them in opposing columns. They
322
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
Table 12.2
<table><tr><td>Appearance:The bucket and the rope&#x27;s view</td><td>Reality:Mr Dendy&#x27;s view</td></tr><tr><td>Equilibrium: (a) Mr Dendy lovesMrs Dendy &amp; (b) she loves the lawyer&#x27;s son</td><td>Disequilibrium: (a) Mr Dendy loves his wife &amp; (b) she loves the lawyer&#x27;s son</td></tr><tr><td>Mediation: Mr Dendy acts in a way &#x27;not usual for a village man&#x27; [35]: he picks a bunch of flowers and gives them to his wife</td><td>Mediation: Mr Dendy wants (a) but not (b)</td></tr><tr><td>Disequilibrium: Mr Dendy kills himself</td><td>Equilibrium: Mr Dendy kills himself</td></tr></table>
represent two alternative views of the events of the story, which we can see as related on the one hand to appearance (the bucket and the rope's take on the events), and on the other hand to reality (Mr Dendy's view of events, which seems schematically close to ours – which is why we label them as 'reality'). We will try to construct an overall interpretation of the story by comparing these two 'thematic' columns.
If we first compare the initial row, where we have used '&' to represent logical conjunction (which can be realised by 'and' or 'but'), we can see that what is equilibrium for the bucket and the rope is disequilibrium for Mr Dendy. This reflects the fact that the combined propositions in the equilibrium line are opposed according to the social schemata we assume we share with Mr Dendy (we would most naturally use 'but' for '&' above), but not for the bucket and the rope (who would be happier with 'and'). This is because in the bucket and the rope's utilitarian view, unlike Mr Dendy's, it is natural to want to give pleasure to as many people as possible in all circumstances. The fact that the bucket and the rope share such a view can be seen in the following extracts, where they approve of Betty's actions towards the lawyer's son:
She had a yielding kindness, but no wickedness in her. She showed her good nature to a young man, the son of a lawyer [...]. The lawyer's son looked unhappy and she allowed him to kiss her, while Mr Dendy was out of the way.' [28]
[...] he should have been glad to see that the lawyer's son thought her as nice as he did.' [29]
'His wife made him [Mr Dendy] so [happy],' said the rope 'and feeling her success with him she naturally wished to make another happy too.' [47]
323
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
The same idea is expressed later, in paragraphs [49], [50], [53], [54] and [55]. The bucket and the rope approve of Betty's attitude towards the lawyer's son, but clearly do not understand Mr Dendy's despair -- a more typically human reaction -- when he finds out what is going on.
The mediation line is different in the two columns because the bucket and the rope think that it is the bunch of flowers that cause Mr Dendy to commit suicide, not his unhappiness about the affair between his wife and the lawyer's son, as we do. This, together with the different interpretations of the logical connector '&' in the first line, which we have already commented on, constitutes the difference between the appearance and reality views of the events in the TAW of 'the bucket and the rope'. But there is also an irony here, in that, although the bucket and the rope get it wrong, they also get it right by accident. When Mr Dendy picks the flowers, he does so to give them to his wife. Hence what is an inexplicable occurrence in the column for the bucket and the rope counts as an indication of the love Mr Dendy has for Mrs Dendy expressed in the first proposition of the first row of the Reality column, prompting his suicidal action.
Just as the first line of the above table is equilibrium for the bucket and the rope but disequilibrium for Mr Dendy, the last line, which presents Mr Dendy's suicide identically in both columns, shows opposite interpretations in the appearance and reality columns. But it is also worth noticing a slight oddity here in Mr Dendy's attitude. If accounts of domestic violence are to be believed, most people who find out about a spouse having an affair are likely to strike out, literally or metaphorically, at their spouse or their spouse's lover, not at themselves. So although Mr Dendy's attitude is mainly like the one we expect in schematic terms, it is not in this respect.
A comparison of the two columns with different ideological viewpoints raises the issue of which ideology is preferable, particularly when we remember the oddity of Mr Dendy taking his unhappiness out on himself, rather than his wife or her lover. The view of the bucket and the rope is perfectly logical in its own terms: Mr Dendy's self-destruction is an unusual form of behaviour, and this must be explained in relation to the only other unusual way he had behaved: his making and giving the posy to Betty. And in a sense this is the correct conclusion, as the nosegay appears to show his unusually deep feelings for his wife, which in turn explains his suicide. But the human view is also logical in its own terms. This same self-destruction, in the bottom line of Table 12.2, leaves intact proposition (b) of line 1: 'she loves the lawyer's son'. So Mr Dendy's deep feeling for this wife, as manifested in the posy-giving, leads to his sacrifice of himself, leaving Betty's love for the lawyer's son to enjoy.
As the bucket and the rope's direct speech is predominant in the text, the story arguably allows us to infer that there is no necessary reason to equate
324
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
love or marriage with exclusive sexual relations, thus problematising (or mediating, in Lévi-Strauss's terms) two views which are richly paradoxical for many because they are held simultaneously.
12.3.7 Fictional worlds and viewpoint
In the previous section we have used Ryan's (1991) account of possible worlds in fiction, where she distinguished between the private world of the characters (K-worlds, W-worlds, and so on) and the TAW (textual actual world) – what in Chapter 5 we called 'the mock-reality'. In this section we concentrate on how we construct the TAW as we read, and how we arrive at a particular viewpoint. This story has a fable-like TAW because the set of human-like characters is extended to include the bucket and the rope (who can see, talk, reason, have views, and so on), but in all other respects appear to obey the natural and psychological laws of the real world. In this respect it obeys Ryan's (1991, pp. 48–60) principle of minimal departure of the TAW from the actual world (AW).
Below we will mainly use the 'text worlds' approach of Paul Werth and his followers in order to spell out aspects of the TAW. At the same time, we will rely on the relevant work of Emmott (1997), deictic shift theory, and more traditional stylistic work. Werth's text world analysis is preferred here to possible worlds and mental spaces theory, as Werth's approach has so far been applied to stories in more detail. To save space, however, we have to present our text world analysis in informal terms. Werth's style of analysis involves producing diagrams for each text world and sub-world, spelling out in considerable detail the various text-building elements of each (as indicated textually), and the relations among those worlds. We will concentrate on aspects of the story that can be related fairly directly to meaning and effect.
A. The opening of the story
As those who propose deictic shift analysis have pointed out, when we sit down to read we have our own deictic centre, based on ourselves and our space/time coordinates as we begin reading, but it does not take long before we have been 'pushed' (Galbraith 1995) mentally to a new deictic centre within the world we are reading about (and so on into the various sub-worlds of the story as it develops). Werth terms these worlds 'text worlds' (since not all worlds we read about are fictional) and points out that referential and deictic items in the text are crucial in defining the complex interrelated set of text worlds we 'create' as we read.
Our story begins with a third-person narration which locates it, in the first two sentences, in a shed somewhere near a village called Shelton containing a dead man hanging by a rope from a beam, with a bucket lying on its side underneath:
325
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
A bucket once lay upon its side in a little shed, that was a short way down a by-lane, near to the village of Shelton. [1]
This bucket, a large one, had been kicked over by a man who had hanged himself up by the neck, by means of an odd piece of rope, that he had tied to a strong beam. [2]
Figure 12.3 is a diagrammatic 'picture' of the scene created by these sentences:
![](images/page_18_0.jpg)

Figure 12.3
This picture is constructed mainly through noun phrases and prepositional phrases specifying objects, places and a human being ('a bucket', 'upon its side in a little shed that was a short way down a by-lane, near to the village of Shelton', 'a man who had hanged himself by the neck, by means of an odd piece of rope that he had tied to a strong beam'). The deictic shift theorists would say that these locational deictic expressions construct the deictic field within which the reader positions herself. By the time we have listed them all, there is little which is not quoted from the first two
326
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
sentences, showing their key role in developing what Labov and Waletzky (1967) would call the orientation phase of the story, as well as the TAW in Ryan's terms. But there is also some inferencing work to be done, to integrate these textual details with schematic knowledge. We know, for example, that the man stood on the bucket before kicking it out from underneath himself. Death by hanging requires the feet to be clear of the ground, and the kicking away of the bucket is an obvious method of achieving this. Already we can see, as Werth and others have emphasised, the key role that inference plays in helping readers construct fictional worlds on the basis of textual information.
In terms of viewpoint, however, the TAW which we have now established is depicted rather idiosyncratically, focusing first of all on the bucket and its position, and not that of the man. The bucket is the subject and topic of the first two sentences, and the information about the suicide is presented in a defining relative clause postmodifying 'man' in the explanation of how the bucket came to be on its side, and is thus downgraded in informational terms (cf section 7.5.1). What is odd about this is that, in spite of the title, we still expect the story, like most stories, to be about people (cf Fairley 1988). But the information about Mr Dendy (which is very dramatic, after all) is not the topic of the sentence and is presented in a subordinate, backgrounding structure, as if it is not salient or important. In this description there is thus already a mismatch in our schematic assumptions about what should count as the figure and what should count as the ground (cf Stockwell 2002, Chapter 2). In terms of Figure 12.3, the textually foregrounded part of the picture is the bucket, not the hanging man, and so the very beginning of the story makes us concentrate not on the dead man, how awful the suicide was and what drove him to kill himself, but on the location and orientation of one of the instruments he used. All this conveys an idiosyncratic, almost ironic, slant on the contents of the TAW from the viewpoint of the third-person narrator, whose viewpoint we, as non-resisting readers (cf section 8.3), work to share.
Sentence/paragraph 3 continues this unusual, offhand approach to the suicide:
The man's name, who had hanged himself was Mr Dendy, who rented a few pleasant fields that he used to plough happily, and, besides keeping a few good cows, he fattened some nice pigs. [3]
Its topic is 'the man's name who had hanged himself' (rather than the man himself), and the end-weighted information describes an apparently happy life, which thus gives no indication whatsoever of what drove him to suicide. Each aspect of the description given involves positive evaluative adjectives and adverbs: he had 'pleasant fields' that he 'ploughed happily', 'good cows' and he 'fattened nice pigs'. In this context even the verb 'fattened'
327
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
is easily understood as being a positive thing (success in what a farmer is meant to do), even though it is not a straightforwardly evaluative term.
We might now expect some more direct information about the dead man, but in the rest of this initial section of third-person narrative we move instead to the shared mental world of the bucket and the rope, and their wish to understand Mr Dendy, their master, as part of their more general desire to 'study men' [6]. In this respect Werth's and Ryan's two approaches overlap. In Werth's terms this is a desire world, part of what he would see as a character-based attitudinal sub-world, and in Ryan's terms we have moved from the TAW to a W-world, which is also character-based. Our view of Mr Dendy is curiously distanced, as if the bucket and the rope have no concern for human suffering and emotion, but are motivated solely by intellectual curiosity.
B. The text-world structure of the rest of the story
The rest of the story, as we pointed out in section 12.3.3, is told through the direct speech of the bucket and the rope, and so we have to discern the reasons for Mr Dendy's suicide through the 'unreliable narration' provided within the direct speech. Within this direct speech we move in the first speech paragraph (which the bucket utters) from a verbal instantiation of the bucket and rope's joint desire world (with present and present perfect tenses and the interrogative form) to another character-accessible sub-world, a flashback via a generalised memory which the bucket shares with the rope:
Perhaps you can remember as well as I the joyful day when we were first purchased, which happened to be the very day before Mr Dendy was married. [9]
The move from present to past tense and the time specification indicate a flashback, the place location of which is then specified in later paragraphs by definite reference. In text world terms, what happens from now on is complex, in the sense that we move fairly swiftly from one sub-world to another, sometimes at the same level, and sometimes at lower levels, embedded inside the flashback sub-world. And for some time the flashback sub-world and the other sub-worlds are not about Mr Dendy, thus frustrating further the reader's wish to find out why he killed himself. This is the text world equivalent of a 'shaggy dog story' as, in deictic shift theory terms (Galbraith 1995, Stockwell 2002, p. 47), we are 'pushed' down from one deictic centre to another and are, with very few exceptions, preventing from 'popping' back up to the TAW with which the story opens.
Below we present the text world structure in a rather simplified form, before discussing it in more detail. Each indentation in Figure 12.4 indicates that the sub-world referred to is embedded in the world immediately above it and, as before, the square-bracketed references indicates the
328
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
relevant paragraphs of the story. The bucket and the rope are abbreviated as B and R respectively, to save space. We have only represented the worlds presented, showing which other worlds they are embedded in. We do not indicate sentence-by-sentence 'toggling' between an embedded world and the world it is embedded in (for example, where sentences relating straightforwardly to the Flashback 1 level below are interspersed with some inferred minor character sub-worlds). Nevertheless, even with our somewhat simplified presentation, it can be seen that in text-world terms, this very short story turns out to be rather complex, with some sub-worlds being created for just a few words (e.g. those of the grand military gentleman and the lady in [17]):
Narrated text world (TAW in Ryan's terms): the scene in the shed [1-3]
The bucket and the rope's (B&R's) desire sub-world (Ryan's wish-world) [4-8]
B&R's shared discourse world (their direct speech) [9 onwards]
Flashback 1 (R in the shop window and what it sees) [14–30]
Flashback 2 (R remembers its childhood) [14]
Inferred inner world of 'the grand military gentleman' [17]
Inferred inner world of the 'lady' [17]
Inferred inner world of the 'young and pleasing girls' [19]
Inferred inner world of the 'poor debtor' [20]
Inferred inner world of Betty [28]
Flashback 3 (B remembers being purchased) [31]
Flashback 4 (B being carried by Mr Dendy to collect swill) [31]
R rejects the 'sour garbage' hypothesis world re the suicide [32]
R rejects the 'heavy bucket' hypothesis world re the suicide [32]
Flashback 5 (B being carried by Mr Dendy again) [33–34]
Mr Dendy's discourse world [33]
Flashback 6 (B remembers the nosegay incident) [35–39]
Flashback 7 (R throttling Mr Dendy in his suicide) [43]
R proposes the Mr Dendy's negative attitude hypothesis for the
suicide [45] (which the bucket rejects [46] and the rope agrees [47])
Flashback 8 (Betty's unfaithful behaviour) [49–58]
Betty's discourse world [49]
Betty's inner world [51]
B's hypothesis about Mr Dendy's inner world (which is then
rejected) [54-55]
B's 'hungry man' hypothesis [56]
B's nosegay hypothesis [60]
Figure 12.4
Apart from the overall text-world complexity of the story, we can see that by the time the bucket and rope begin to address what the reader is most interested in, their hypotheses concerning Mr Dendy's suicide, we are 32 paragraphs and 12 worlds into the story, many of the worlds being embedded four levels down from the initial TAW. In terms of Emmott's work on
329
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
narrative comprehension (1997, pp. 103–132), the dead Mr Dendy is only 'primed' in the contextual frame of the scene in the shed (the TAW), but we have to monitor the rest of the text for matters relating to his suicide, as we move through the contextual frames associated with all the other text worlds created in the story. The world-structure of the story, like the way in which the scene is initially presented, thus goes strongly against our normal expectations. Given the overall brevity of the story, it is clear that, although many of these embedded worlds take up just a few words, they are nonetheless disruptive of our wish to understand why Mr Dendy killed himself.
Initially in Flashback 1 the rope is in a shop window, and can observe happenings in two directions, outside and inside the shop. \( ^{3} \)  Paragraphs [16]–[21] describe what the rope saw outside the window, and we also move, presumably via the rope's inferencing abilities, inside the mental worlds of some of the people it observes. We are given brief pieces of knowledge about the attitudinal and desire worlds of a 'grand military gentleman', a lady, 'two young and pleasing girls' and a debtor, as well as being given external descriptions of other characters before, finally, the young Mr Dendy and his prospective wife are introduced externally in [21] ('The man was Mr Dendy, and she was to be his wife').
Next, the bucket intervenes, with a flashback based on the initial flashback:
'Her arms had been opened to another before him,' murmured the bucket. [22]
The rope comments on this flashback and then resumes its own flashback narrative, concentrating on Mr Dendy and his fiancée, first outside the shop and then inside it.
All the time the reader, of course, is monitoring the text for information which is relatable to the initial narrative disequilibrium, Mr Dendy's suicide. The first conceivable possibility finally occurs in paragraph [26]:
'Mr Dendy was about twenty-nine years old then, and the young girl about eighteen,' remarked the bucket. [25]
'So she was' said the rope, 'but it is curious to think now what she did next. While Mr Johnson and Mr Dendy were talking, she coiled and uncoiled me, and then, in her girlish amusement, for she looked at him lovingly, she made a running noose of me, slipped it over the master's head, and pulled it tight.' [26]
The fact that his prospective wife makes the rope into a noose and puts it round Mr Dendy's neck cannot be related to his suicide in a direct way, but like the 'death' semantic prosody we perceived in the title phrases 'the bucket' and 'the rope' in section 12.3.2 (see also section 12.3.7 below),
330
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
it can be seen as a possible prefiguring of what is to happen later in the chronological narrative. She has already been described in [21], via an attitudinal sub-world inferred by the rope, as having 'the sweetest of natures, that would be unlikely, for the fear of being cruel, to refuse anything a man might ask of her'. This, related to the bucket's secondary flashback in [22], suggests that Betty could well be like Amaryllis, the girl of easy virtue in Campion's poem (cf section 12.3.4). Moreover, in [28] she is described as letting the lawyer's son kiss her 'while Mr Dendy was out of the way'.
From [32] onwards we are presented with a series of hypothetical worlds giving possible explanations for Mr Dendy's motivation in killing himself, all of which are either not taken up or are explicitly rejected, until we get to the final hypothesis, concerning the nosegay, proposed by the bucket in an exclamatory utterance that appears to end the conversation, leading to the possibility that the rope has accepted the bucket's proposal. Whether we think that the rope has indeed accepted the bucket's suggestion is dependent to some degree on the character and general attitude of the rope, to which we will return in section 12.3.9 below.
C. Other aspects of viewpoint: naming and discourse presentation
We have seen how the topic-comment structure of the first three sentences of the story expressed a viewpoint in terms of interest which goes against what we would normally expect from a description of a man who has just committed suicide. Alongside this, there is a frustration of the reader's expectation, and a felt need (because of the disequilibrium) for an explanation of the suicide. We do not have the space to specify in detail the viewpoint-related factors in every paragraph, but it will be helpful to make some more comments about viewpoint in the story as a whole.
First, it is worth noting that in both the narrative and the direct speech of the bucket and the rope, the default expression used to refer to Mr Dendy is the 'title + last name' formulation we have just used. We never learn what his first name is. The narrator uses it seven times and the bucket and the rope 24 times. Even allowing for the more formal naming and address habits of 1927 (when the story was first published), this means that both the narrator and the bucket and the rope express a relatively distant, formal social relationship with the character, one which the reader is also likely to adopt, given the third-person narrator's (and hence, by conventional inference, the author's) stance (cf sections 8.3–8.5). There are some departures from this default expression, in addition to the standard pronominal forms he, him, etc. Before naming him, the narrator uses 'a man who had hanged himself by the neck' (twice) and 'the man' (three times), and the bucket and the rope use 'our master' three times and 'the master' twice, thus expressing their functional relation to Mr Dendy. This consistently distanced form of reference to the character helps to dissuade us from empathising with him, much as the strategy at the opening of the story
331
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
does, leading us to consider Mr Dendy and his plight more dispassionately. All the other characters apart from Mr Dendy's wife are referred to with the 'title + last name' formulation or something even more distancing in type, and Betty's lover is kept even more distanced from us: he is never given a name (and we never enter his mental world), but is referred to as a 'young man' or in relation to another person in the story ('the lawyer's son', Betty's 'friend'), which distances him from us even further.
The narrator does not explicitly refer to Mr Dendy's wife (or indeed any of the other characters) at all. We learn everything about her through the direct speech of the bucket and the rope. Their favourite reference form for her is the first-name form 'Betty' which they use six times, and which expresses a familiarity towards her that they appear not to feel for their master. No doubt this can be partly attributed to her young age. But because 'Betty' is the only name given to the character, that reference form becomes the default, and indeed it feels most natural to us to refer to her as 'Betty' too, in spite of the fact that the third-person narrator never uses it. This seems to position us as closer to, and potentially more sympathetic towards, her than her husband, for whom 'Mr Dendy' is the only name available to us. The bucket and the rope also refer to Betty seven times as 'Mr Dendy's wife', thus expressing what they see as her most important relation to Mr Dendy, as well as using 'woman' and 'girl'.
We have already noted (section 12.3.3) that the story divides clearly into a narration section and a direct speech dialogue section, and that everything we learn about the events leading up to Mr Dendy's suicide, including his relations with all the other characters, are seen through the bucket and the rope's direct speech discourse world. It is this distancing, via discoursal embedding, which allows the possibility of the two interpretations of what the bucket and the rope tell us, leading to the appearance/ reality theme and interpretation referred to earlier (sections 12.3.1 and 12.3.4). The speech of the bucket and the rope effectively becomes a shared embedded narration (they use 'we' to refer to themselves eight times as well as 'I' (29 times) and 'you' (12 times).
In addition to the dominant role of direct speech, there are some other points to make about discourse presentation in the story. Beginning with the first eight paragraphs of narration, we can see that the narrator establishes the wish world of the bucket and the rope partly through the presentation of their thoughts and mind states. Thought presentation analysis thus constitutes one of the ways in which we can provide evidence (in Werth's terms) for character-accessible sub-worlds. In the last half of the final sentence of [4], the first clause ('would speak of all that Mr Dendy did') is a narrator's presentation of a speech act (cf section 10.1.3) representing the bucket and rope's habitual speech act behaviour, and then the rest of the sentence slips into a form which is ambiguous between indirect speech and indirect thought presentation:
332
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
[...] [they would] endeavour to find out as best they might a reason for his actions. [4]
By way of contrast, the narrator tells us virtually nothing directly about Mr Dendy's thoughts. The only possible case of presentation of an internal view of Mr Dendy from the perspective of the third-person narrator occurs when we are told that he ploughed his fields 'happily' in sentence 3. This is extremely minimal internal narration (cf Semino and Short 2004, pp. 132–135). By paragraph [7], on the other hand, the bucket and the rope are making weak inferences about Mr Dendy's inner world:
Now and again the two friends had been a little puzzled by Mr Dendy, who did not often act as they would have expected him to, for sometimes he would seem to be troubled, when, according to the bucket's ideas of cause and effect, there was no reason for him to be so. [7]
Here, the first clause is internal narration concerning the mind state of the bucket and the rope and the third clause shows Mr Dendy's internal mind state as inferred by the bucket and the rope (cf Semino and Short 2004, pp. 135–137), with appropriate hedging via the non-factive verb 'seem'. Given that the bucket and the rope cannot see into Mr Dendy's mind in the way that an omniscient third-person narrator could, we would expect the inferred thought presentation in the story to be minimal, and the absence of more direct forms of thought presentation for Mr Dendy means that even though he apparently kills himself for love, it is difficult for us to become emotionally involved with his plight. Indeed, in this respect Mr Dendy is treated rather like a minor character – for the mind states of a number of minor characters are also established through inferred narration of inner states, for example the 'grand military gentleman' in [17], who the rope tells us 'became prouder than ever'.
Within the bucket and the rope's direct speech there is not much embedded presentation of the speech or writing of the other characters, which is consistent with Powys's 'withdrawal of sympathy' tactic that we have just outlined. However, there is one example of embedded direct speech presentation (eDS - cf Semino and Short 2004, pp. 171-182), and one example of embedded direct writing presentation (eDW), both of which become foregrounded through internal deviation (cf section 2.6). The instance of eDS is in the first sentence of [49] 'You look so well in it [...]', when Betty encourages Mr Dendy to go to church so that she can carry on her secret affair with the lawyer's son. It thus highlights her duplicitous behaviour. The instance of eDW is the quotation from Thomas Campion's 'Amaryllis' in [57]. The quotation, as an allusion to the poem, prompts us to see a parallel between Betty and Amaryllis, who gives her favours so freely, underlining Betty's unfaithfulness to her husband. Hence we are
333
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
likely to view Betty critically although in naming terms, as we have already seen, we are brought closer to her than to Mr Dendy.
12.3.8 Textual analysis in terms of lexis, grammar and meaning
The style of the story is rather simple and straightforward, as we would expect of a fable. But one general feature deserves comment – a tendency to archaism. We notice this in a number of words and expressions that strike the twenty-first-century reader, and would have struck even the reader of Powys's day, as somewhat quaint, old-fashioned and literary: for example, 'purchased' [13], 'the folk' [16] 'our master' [24], 'a winning gait' [28], 'a merry jest' [29], 'we became acquainted' [31], 'tarry', 'droll story', 'a-walking'. This is in keeping with the tone of a fable as a traditional story handing down folk wisdom. It is significant that this story contains no references that would enable us to place the events described historically. Except for the names 'Mr Dendy' and 'Betty', which tend to place it later than the Middle Ages, the story could have taken place during any century. The tendency to archaism underlines the timelessness of the story.
We now turn more generally to lexical patterning, and begin our observations, paradoxically, with the last word of 'The Bucket and the Rope', 'nosegay', which is arguably also the most striking word in the story. It is foregrounded not just by its textual position but also by its rarity. We found no occurrences of 'nosegay(s)' in written British English corpora amounting to 2.8 million words of texts from the 1930–1991 period, and only eight occurrences in the 100-million-word British National Corpus, dating from the 1990s. This may suggest that this word is also fairly old-fashioned and may already have been so when Powys was writing. Indeed, the Oxford English Dictionary (OED) marks it as 'archaic'.
If we look at the semantic prosodies (cf Louw 1997) of 'nosegay' in these corpus entries we find that the word has opposing associations. As small bunches of flowers, nosegays are clearly pretty and sweet-smelling, and some text examples relate straightforwardly to this positive image, as do all of the entries for the word in the OED. But nosegays were also used historically to ward off evil smells (for example, during the plague and at funerals), and other text examples relate to this function. This 'Janus-faced' aspect of 'nosegay' relates interestingly to the life/death theme of the story and also to the appearance/reality theme discussed in section 12.3.4. The bucket and the rope see the nosegay in a negative light (possibly as causing Mr Dendy's death), whereas we see it initially as positive (when Mr Dendy offers it to his wife as a token of his affection) but then can also see it negatively as a 'sign' of that affection and therefore as a harbinger of his suicide.
Another case of lexical foregrounding is the collocation occurring when Betty is referred to as a cure for Mr Dendy's 'burden of his desire':
334
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
'He married as you know, a woman, a creature created to ease a man of the heavy burden of his desire, a burden as troublesome to carry as a kicking ass.' [10]
We will not comment on the bucket's sexist attitude, which is obvious enough, but instead examine the foregrounded effect of 'burden of desire' – an instantiation of what Lakoff and Turner (1989, pp. 25–26) call the 'life is a burden' or 'difficulty is a burden' metaphor. 'Desire' can be either positive (e.g. the desire to help others) or negative (e.g. the desire for power), and the BNC examples for 'desire for' reflect this split. But 'burden' has an obviously negative semantic prosody, thus forcing a negative semantic association for 'desire' here. The large majority of the BNC entries for 'burden(s) of' have a negative prosody and the examples below are taken from the first few entries. 'Burden of' is typically completed by heavy loads (e.g. '865 kilograms') or feelings or other objects and attitudes which are negative (e.g. 'guilt'). Hence in this story sexual desire is portrayed negatively, and it is interesting to note that the story emphasises the negative connotation of 'burden' elsewhere. 'Burden' is also associated with the 'kicking ass' simile which immediately follows 'burden of desire', locally reinforcing the 'difficulty is a burden' metaphor. In the story Betty is at first portrayed as good for Mr Dendy, and his desire to marry her seems at first to be a good thing. But the semantic prosody of 'burden of desire', like Betty's making a noose of the rope in the shop and slipping it over Mr Dendy's head, prefigures the change in her relation with Mr Dendy just before their marriage.
In addition to his use of particular foregrounded words and phrases, Powys displays a more general attention to lexical patterning in bringing out the themes of his story. We noted earlier (see pp. 327–8) how positive evaluative terms ('pleasant', 'happily', 'good', 'nice') accompany the mention of Mr Dendy when his dead body is first described hanging in the shed, contrasting with our normal reactions to suicide. This lexical pattern in the first three sentences could be a signal to alert the reader to other lexical patterns in the story, some of which could be related to the first one. The story has many positive words and phrases which relate to Mr Dendy (e.g. he would have 'a merry word with the occupants, telling a droll story of country matters for the young girls to smile at' [33] and the bucket refers to him as 'a true and joyful countryman, doing his proper tasks' [42]. Betty, initially, at least, also enjoys positive lexis (e.g. 'she had the sweetest of natures' [21] and 'she looked at him lovingly' [26].
But, more importantly, there are a lot of phrases and clauses (not included in the discussion above) which yoke the good and the bad together syntactically. Some are in simple contrastive constructions (italics = positive and boldening = negative):
335
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
loves and hates [4]
their sorrows and joys [6]
but as the story unfolds the good/bad relations become more complex, for example:
He learns to brave the keenest wind without a shudder, and cares nothing when the rain soaks him to the skin. No weather daunted Mr Dendy, and the more he bore with the storms outside the pleasanter was his parlour, with its cheerful light, and the warm presence of a wife who loved him.' [39]
In the above examples, the underlined items cannot be classified straightforwardly on the basis of their lexical meaning. Three of the words involved, 'shudder', 'cares' and 'daunted' are arguably negative in associative terms, but they are involved in grammatical negation which reverses the overall semantic prosody. In the following example, pragmatic factors outweigh prototypical lexical assumptions in overall understanding:
She had a yielding kindness, but no wickedness in her. [28]
'A yielding kindness' looks at first sight positive. But it is contrasted with 'no wickedness' here, which in turn involves a negation turning a negative prosody into a positive one. Hence, if 'yielding kindness' is being contrasted with a positive, it needs to be interpreted as having a negative prosody itself, and in context it clearly refers to the fact that, like Amaryllis, Betty seems not able to resist the attentions of men.
We have just seen that the lexical patterning in the story is easily interpreted as connected to the story's positive/negative and appearance/ reality themes. The appearance/reality theme is also indicated by other lexical patterns. Not surprisingly, non-factive verbs of perception are quite prominent. For example, 'seem' occurs seven times in the story, 'thought' five times, 'appear' twice, 'look' (in its 'appear' meaning) five times, 'believe' twice, and 'fancy' twice; and the modal verbs 'can' and 'could' appear three and nine times respectively. Modal verbs connected with obligation and certainty are also reasonably prominent: 'should' appears nine times, along with one 'must' (in the final sentence, where the bucket's certainty is clearly unfounded), and one 'ought'. The modal adverb 'certainly' also appears three times.
Finally, there are also a number of expressions which indicate that various aspects of the behaviour of Mr Dendy are surprising or intriguing for the bucket and the rope:
(a) Mr Dendy's movements as well as his behaviour had always been of great interest to the bucket and the rope [4]
336
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'

(b) Both were interested in any kind of life that was not like themselves [5]
(c) Now and again the two friends had been a little puzzled by Mr Dendy, who did not often act as they would have expected him to [ . . . ] [7]
(d) 'Is it not a little curious if not altogether surprising,' observed the bucket, 'that we should have been put to so sad a use in helping our good master die? [...]' [9]
(e) '[...] and a few of them particularly attracted my notice [...]' [16]
(f) '[...] it is curious to think now what she did next.' [26]
(g) 'Only once,' observed the bucket sadly, 'did I notice Mr Dendy act in a way that was not usual for a village man. [...]' [35]
Many of these expressions show the bucket and the rope noticing what they see as deviant behaviour and trying to understand it; but because they do not share human assumptions, they are not able to understand.
12.3.9 Characterisation
In the final section of this analysis we will look at characterisation in 'The Bucket and the Rope'. As already noted at the beginning of this chapter, Culpeper (2001, p. 28) points out the need to combine top-down (schemas etc.) and bottom-up (textual signals of various kinds) processing to arrive at an understanding of characters and characterisation (as indeed we have to do in relation to most aspects of textual understanding). In spite of its brevity, which gives little opportunity of character development or character change in the story, 'The Bucket and the Rope' illustrates well the top-down/bottom-up processing distinction.
Because we see little of Mr Dendy and Betty directly, there is not much opportunity for bottom-up processing in establishing what they are like. As we don't see them interact very much, we have to rely mainly on (i) what the bucket and the rope tell us about them (which, as we have seen, is unreliable), and (ii) schematic assumptions that we, as readers, bring to the text. Intriguingly, although we recognise that they are unreliable narrators, we tend not to reject what they say about the human characters unless it contradicts our schematic assumptions.
For the bucket and the rope, on the other hand, this top-down/bottom-up processing situation is reversed. We don't possess character schemata for talking buckets and ropes and how they might behave, and so we can only make bottom-up inferences. And apart from talking and thinking (which we mainly see through their talk), they don't actually carry out any actions. These inanimate objects apparently cannot move (cf Ryan's 1991, pp. 46–80 principle of minimal departure), even though they are able to talk and reason, and so we have to interpret their characters entirely on the basis of what they say.
337
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
A. Mr Dendy
We know from the omniscient third-person narrator that Mr Dendy is 'a simple countryman' [6], which invokes schemata in relation to country folk: that they are honest, naive, hard-working, have a traditional mindset (e.g. concerning sexual relations and marriage) and so on. We know that he is naturally happy and contented because the narrator says that he ploughed his fields 'happily' [3] and the bucket and the rope reinforce this view. The bucket says that he was 'a truly joyful countryman' [42], and that he whistles [32] and talks jokingly to others [33]. When the rope says that he carried the bucket 'jauntily, as if the burden of a few gallons of swill was nothing to so powerful an arm as his' [32], this confirms Mr Dendy's happy disposition and suggests via weak implicature that he is hard-working. He also puts up with all weathers with fortitude [33], [39]. Although he is mainly happy in the story he clearly becomes unhappy just before his suicide. But as we assume that he killed himself because of his wife's activities with the lawyer's son, we reject the rope's view that he killed himself because he 'did not like to see others happy' [45], which in any case is inconsistent with everything else we have been told about him. As Culpeper (2001, pp. 116–119), following Kelley (1972), points out, we can discount individual exceptional examples of behaviour as not contributing to character. For example, such behaviour may be affected by special contextual factors: if we see someone shouting angrily at someone who has assaulted him, we attribute that behaviour more to context than to character. This is especially true if the behaviour contrasts with habitual behaviour we have repeated evidence for, and so Mr Dendy's suicidal behaviour appears to be 'out of character'.
B. Betty
Our view of Betty is even more restricted than our view of Mr Dendy. We only learn about her through the direct speech of the bucket and the rope, much of which reinforces a conventional set of assumptions about attractive young women. She is sweet, loving and playful according to the following descriptions: 'in her girlish amusement, for she looked at him lovingly' [26]; 'she had the sweetest of natures' [21] and 'she seemed just the creature to delight any man with her sweetness' [28]. But at the same time, these positive qualities are mixed with others which are less positive – as we see from the rest of the constructions from which the above quotations come:
She seemed just the creature to delight any man with her sweetness and eagerness for love. [21]
[...] in her girlish amusement, for she looked at him lovingly, she made a running noose of me, slipped it over the master's head, and pulled it tight' [26]
338
<PAGE>
'The Bucket and the Rope'
Anyone could see that she had the sweetest of natures, that would be unlikely, for the fear of being cruel, to refuse anything a man might ask of her. [28]
And of course she does not pass up on the opportunity to turn the lawyer's son's 'sorrow into joy' [50]. Betty also appears to be manipulative, as her foregrounded direct speech indicates, when, somewhat ironically, she persuades her husband to go to church so that she can enjoy her clandestine affair [49]. Betty thus combines two contrasting stereotypes for fictional young women – sweetly attractive and dangerously attractive – and what we are told by the bucket and the rope reinforces these two partly contradictory stereotypes. We do not discount Betty's manipulative behaviour, perhaps because, as Culpeper (2001, p. 145), following Margolin (1983), points out, we tend to generalise from less evidence in fictional texts than in real life, because the knowledge base is complete and smaller – and indeed very small in a short story like this one.
C. The bucket and the rope
Are the bucket and the rope one character or two? They certainly have the same ideological assumptions and have the same difficulties in interpreting human behaviour, but they also exhibit some differences.
The rope talks much more than the bucket (1508 v. 675 words), but this difference is probably not character-related. We can discount it if we notice that around 700 of the rope's words relate to the description of the scene on the day when it was bought by Mr Dendy, when the bucket was not present. Hence this information, which is important for understanding the story, can be produced only by the rope. An examination of the turn-taking patterns in the conversation also reveals no big differences between the bucket and the rope. It is difficult to portray one as more powerful or talkative than the other, for example.
