E. Mind style
The following are useful explorations of mind style (the topic of Chapter 4 with reference to particular authors and works:
BANKS, D. (1992) 'Men, women and transitivity in To the Lighthouse and Farewell to Arms' in: TOSSER, Y. and P. GALLIOU, eds, Mirroirs de la Femm dans la Littérature d'Expression Anglaise, Brest, Groupe de Recherche sur le Roman et la Nouvelle de Langue Anglaise, Université de Bretagne Occidentale.
BOCKTING, I. (1995) Character and Personality in the Novels of William Faulkner Lanham, MD, University Press of America.
HALLIDAY, M.A.K. (1996 [1971]) 'Linguistic function and literary style: a inquiry into the language of William Golding's The Inheritors', in WEBEIJ. (under A. General: Style etc., above), pp. 56–86.
372
<PAGE>
Further reading
SEMINO, E. (2002) 'A cognitive stylistic approach to mind style in narrative fiction', in SEMINO and CULPEPER (under G. Cognitive Stylistics, below), pp. 95–122.
SEMINO, E. and SWINDLEHURST, K. (1996) 'Metaphor and mind style, in One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest', Style 30, 1, 143–166.

G. Cognitive stylistics, including metaphor
Another field that has achieved great significance in the past 25 years is cognitive linguistics, which in turn has led to the development of cognitive stylistics (see section 11.1). The following publications show how the mental models and operations involved in the interpretation of texts can be studied in depth, using Fauconnier's mental spaces and blending theory, and newer, fuller understandings of metaphor. Fauconnier's book is cited below, although it is not concerned with stylistics as such.
DANCYGIER, B. (2005) 'Blending and narrative viewpoint: Jonathan Raban's travels through mental spaces', Language and Literature 14, 2, 99–127.
EMMOTT, C. (1997) Narrative Comprehension: a discourse perspective, Oxford, Oxford University Press.
FAUCONNIER, G. (1985 [1997]) Mental Spaces, Cambridge, MA, MIT Press. [Second edition published by Cambridge University Press, New York.]
FAUCONNIER, G. and TURNER, M. (2002) The Way We Think: conceptual blending and the mind's hidden complexities, New York, Basic Books.
GAVINS, J. and STEEN, G., eds (2003) Cognitive Poetics in Practice, Routledge.
GIBBS, R.W. (1994) The Poetics of Mind, Cambridge, Cambridge University Press.
LAKOFF, G. and TURNER, M. (1989) More than Cool Reason: a field guide to poetic metaphor, Chicago, Chicago University Press.
LANGUAGE AND LITERATURE (2006) 15(1) Special Issue: Blending.
SEMINO, E. (1992) 'Schema theory and the analysis of text worlds in poetry', Language and Literature 4(2), 79–108.
SEMINO, E. and CULPEPER, J., eds (2002) Cognitive Stylistics: language and cognition in text analysis, Amsterdam and Philadelphia, John Benjamins.
SIMON-VANDENBERGEN, A.-M. (1993) 'Speech, music and dehumanisation in George Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four: a linguistic study of metaphors', Language and Literature 2(3), 157–182.
STEEN, G. (1989) Understanding Metaphor in Literature: an empirical approach, Longman.
STOCKWELL, P. (2002) Cognitive Poetics: an introduction, London, Routledge.
TURNER, M. (1996) The Literary Mind, Oxford, Oxford University Press.
ZWAAN, R.A. (1993) Aspects of Literary Comprehension: a cognitive approach, Amsterdam and Philadelphia, Benjamins.
373
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction
H. Point of view, focalisation, and the like
The broad concept of point of view (or viewpoint), discussed in Chapters 5 and 8 of this book, leads us into a more socially-oriented exploration of fictional style. Starting with Wayne Booth's seminal work, the following list shows how more recent publications have explored new angles and approaches, such as those associated with focalisation. This area has been explored in considerable depth by narratologists as well as stylisticians, as reflected in the list below. Many of the books in the Narratology section above also contain chapters on point of view, and the notion of mind style (see Section F above) can arguably be subsumed under viewpoint.

BOOTH, W. (1961 [1983]) The Rhetoric of Fiction, Chicago, Chicago University Press.
CHATMAN, S. and VAN PEER, W. (2001) New Perspectives on Narrative Perspective, Albany, State University of New York Press.
EHRLICH, S. (1990) Point of View: a linguistic analysis of literary style, Routledge.
LANSER, S. (1981) The Narrative Act: point of view in prose fiction, Princeton, NJ, Princeton University Press.
MARGOLIN, U. (1996) 'Telling our story: on "we" literary narratives', Language and Literature 5(2), 115–133.
MCINTYRE, D. (2006) Point of View in Plays: a cognitive stylistic approach to viewpoint in drama and other text-types, Amsterdam and Philadelphia, John Benjamins.
NELLES, W. (1990) 'Getting focalization into focus', Poetics Today 11(2), 365–382.
SASAKI, T. (1994) 'Towards a systematic description of narrative "point of view": an examination of Chatman's theory with an analysis of The Blind Man by D.H. Lawrence', Language and Literature 3(2), 125–138.
SHORT, M., CULPEPER, J. and SEMINO, E. Language and context: Jane Gardam's Bilgewater, in BEX, T., BURKE, M. and STOCKWELL, P., eds (2000) Contextualised Stylistics, Amsterdam and Atlanta, GA, Rodopi.
SIMPSON, P. (1993) Language, Ideology and Point of View, Routledge.
I. Text and discourse orientation: including critical discourse analysis
Taking the social orientation further, this section shows how developments in the domain of discourse analysis have shed new light on literature. One particular field, that of critical discourse analysis (CDA, see section 11.1), has attracted a great deal of attention in the last twenty-five years, harnessing the analysis of such ideological issues as power, identity and gender
374
<PAGE>
Further reading
to the interpretation of fictional texts. The articles by Fairclough and Widdowson below are a small sample of polemic papers illustrating how the 'critical' stance of CDA has itself evoked criticism and debate.

BIRCH, D. (1991) The Language of Drama: critical theory and practice, Macmillan.
CARTER, R. (1996) English as Language: discourse, stylistics and the curriculum, Routledge.
CARTER, R. and SIMPSON, P., eds (1995 [1989]) Language, Discourse and Literature: an introductory reader in discourse stylistics, Routledge. (Quite a lot on prose, conversation in fiction, etc.)
FAIRCLOUGH, N. (1996) 'A reply to Henry Widdowson's "Discourse analysis: a critical view"', Language and Literature 5(1), 49–56.
FAIRCLOUGH, N. (2001 [1992]) Language and Power, Harlow, Pearson Education.
FAIRCLOUGH, N. (2003) Analysing Discourse: textual analysis for social research, Longman.
FOWLER, R. (1981) Literature as Social Discourse: the practice of linguistic criticism, Batsford.
FOWLER, R. (1991) Language in the News: Discourse and Ideology in the press, Routledge.
HUNSTON, S. and THOMPSON, G., eds (2000) Evaluation in Text: authorial stance and the construction of discourse, Oxford, Oxford University Press.
MILLS, S. (1995) Feminist Stylistics, Routledge.
O'HALLORAN, K. (2004) Critical Discourse Analysis and Language Cognition, Edinburgh, Edinburgh University Press.
WEBER, J.J. (1992) Critical Analysis of Fiction: essays in discourse stylistics, Amsterdam, Rodopi. (Deals with a range of fictional classics.)
WIDDOWSON, H.G. (1996) Discourse analysis: a critical view, Language and Literature 4(3), 157–172.
WIDDOWSON, H.G. (1998) 'The theory and practice of critical discourse analysis', Applied Linguistics 19(1), 136–151.
J. Pragmatics and literature
Pragmatics is another sub-discipline of linguistics that has burgeoned in the last 25 years. In Chapters 8 and 9 of this book we tried to show how some tools of pragmatics – speech acts, conversational implicature, politeness theory – could be applied to fictional works. But progress has been made since then, and can be followed up in a number of publications. The list below includes some works from a field called 'literary pragmatics' (e.g. Sell 2000), but not all those who use pragmatic analysis for literature would consider themselves to be part of a literary pragmatics 'school'.
375
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction

HICKEY, L., ed. (1989) The Pragmatics of Style, London, Routledge.
PILKINGTON, A. (2000) Poetics Effects: a relevance theory perspective, Amsterdam, Benjamins.
PRATT, M.L. (1977) Toward a Speech Act Theory of Literary Discourse Bloomington, IN, Indiana University Press.
SELL, R.D., ed. (1991) Literary Pragmatics, Routledge.
SELL, R.D. (2000) Literature as Communication. The Foundations of Mediating Criticism, Benjamins. (Interesting glossary on literary pragmatics, etc.)
VERDONK, P. (1995) 'Words, words, words: a pragmatic and socio-cognitive view of lexical repetition', in Verdonk and Weber (under B: The novel and style in prose writing above), pp. 7–31.
K. Speech, voices and dialogue
In an obvious sense, 'speech' in novels refers to the fictional speech put into the mouths of characters, as already explored in this book in Chapters 5 (section 5.4), 9 and 12. This area of study has been explored further through more specialised publications such as Chapman (1994) below, and through the investigation of style in drama, represented in other publications listed. In an extended sense, terms such as 'dialogue (especially its adjective 'dialogic') and 'voices' have been applied to the 'conversation' between different narrators, characters, genre styles, and so on, which often make a work of fiction a complex of interacting authorial and narratorial presences. A seminal figure here is the Russian philosopher and literary scholar Mikhail Bakhtin, who, like other Soviet-era scholars, was belatedly 'discovered' by the West, and has posthumously influenced stylistic thinking. Although we did not expressly refer to this 'polyphonic' aspect of the language of fiction, arguably much of Chapters 5 and 10 deal with it implicitly, particularly in the concluding section of Chapter 10.
BAKHTIN, M. (1981) The Dialogic Imagination, ed. M. Holquist, and trans C. Emerson and M. Holquist, Austin, University of Texas Press.
BUCK, R.A. and AUSTIN, T.R. (1995) 'Dialogue and power in E.M. Forster's Howards End', in Verdonk and Weber (under B: The novel and style in prose writing above), pp. 63–77.
BURTON, D. (1980) Dialogue and Discourse, Routledge and Kegan Paul.
CHAPMAN, R. (1994) Forms of Speech in Victorian Fiction, Longman.
CULPEPER, J., SHORT, M. and VERDONK, P., eds (1998) Exploring the Language of Drama: from text to context, Routledge.
DENTITH, S. (1995) Bakhtinian Thought: an introductory reader, Routledge.
HERMAN, V. (1995) Dramatic Discourse: dialogue as interaction in plays Routledge.
376
<PAGE>
Further reading
THOMAS, B.E. (1997) "It's good to talk"? An analysis of a telephone conversation from Evelyn Waugh's Vile Bodies', Language and Literature 6(2), 105–119.
TOOLAN, M. (1985) 'Analysing fictional dialogue', Language and Communication 5(3), 193–206.
I. Presentation of speech and thought
The following publications are a sample of the extensive work on speech and thought representation that has been continuing as a strong theme in the stylistics of fiction.
BRAY, J. (2003) The Epistolary Novel: representations of consciousness, Routledge. COOPER, A. (1994) "Folk-speech" and "book English": re-presentation of dialect in Hardy's novels', Language and Literature 3(1), 21–41.
FLUDERNIK, M. (1993) The Fictions of Language and the Languages of Fiction: the linguistic representation of speech and consciousness, Routledge.
PAGE, N. (1973) Speech in the English Novel, Longman.
PALMER, A. (2004) Fictional Minds, Lincoln, NE, University of Nebraska Press.
SEMINO, E. and SHORT, M. (2004) Corpus Stylistics: Speech, Writing and Thought Presentation in a Corpus of English Writing, Routledge.
SHORT, M., SEMINO, E. and WYNNE, M. (2002) 'Revisiting the notion of faithfulness in discourse presentation using a corpus approach', Language and Literature 11(4), 325–356.

M. Computational, quantitative and corpus methods
For obvious reasons, the computer today is a much more powerful tool for the study of literary style than it was in 1980. For example, there is now a community of 'corpus stylisticators' working with large collections of texts on computer, giving a much more solidly empirical basis to the study of style as discussed in Chapters 2 and 3. The short list below shows something of the range of topics and techniques covered. Semino and Short (2004), referred to in the last section, also use a corpus-based approach, but based on hand-annotation rather than automatic annotation using specially developed software. Biber et al. (1998) and Kennedy are useful introductions to corpus linguistics.
BIBER, D., CONRAD, S. and REPPEN, R. (1998) Corpus Linguistics: investigating language structure and use, Cambridge, Cambridge University Press.
FINCH, A.M. (1995) 'The imagery of a myth: computer-assisted research on literature', Style 19(4), 511–521. (Followed by a comment by L. Milic.)
377
<PAGE>
Style in Fiction

HARDY, D.E. (2003) Narrating Knowledge in Flannery O'Connor's Fiction Colombia, University of South Carolina Press.
HOOVER, D. (1999) Language and Style in The Inheritors, Lanham, MD University Press of America.
HORI, M. (2004) Investigating Dickens' Style: a collocational analysis, Basingstoke Palgrave.
KENNEDY, G. (1998) An Introduction to Corpus Linguistics, Longman.
MILIC, L.T. (1967) A Quantitative Approach to the Style of Jonathan Swift, The Hague, Mouton.
MILIC, L.T. and SLANE, S. (1994) 'Quantitative aspects of genre in the Century of Prose corpus' Style 28(1), 309–340.
STUBBS, M. (2005) Conrad in the computer: examples of quantitative stylistic methods, Language and Literature, 14(1), 5–24.
N. Style of particular authors
One way in which the stylistics of fiction has advanced is in detailed studies of individual authors' work, as illustrated by the following titles. Hardy (2003), Hoover (1999) and Hori (2004), referred to in the above section, are also studies of individual authors. Corpus stylistics works well on large data sets like groups of novels.
ATTRIDGE, D. (2000) Joyce Effects: on language, theory and history, Cambridge, Cambridge University Press.

BUCK, R.A. and AUSTIN, T.R. (1995) 'Dialogue and power in E.M. Forster's Howards End', in Verdonk and Weber (under B: The novel and style in prose writing above), pp. 63–77.
CHATMAN, S. (1972) The Later Style of Henry James, Oxford, Blackwell.
FOWLER, R. (1995) The Language of George Orwell, Basingstoke, Macmillan.
PAGE, N. (1972) The Language of Jane Austen, Oxford, Blackwell.
PAXMAN, D. (2003) 'Metaphor and knowledge in George Eliot's Middlemarch', Metaphor and Symbol 18(2), 107–124.
STOKES, M. (1991) The Language of Jane Austen, London, Macmillan.
WALES, K. (1992) The Language of James Joyce, Basingstoke, Macmillan.
WATT, I. (1969 [1960]) 'The first paragraph of The Ambassadors: an explication', reprinted in LOVE, G.A. and PAYNE, M. (1969) Contemporary Essays on Style, Glenview, IL, Scott Foresman, pp. 266–283.
O. Particular topics: characterisation, creativity, etc.
One cannot always assign new work in stylistics and poetics to neat categories. Perhaps one sign of the vitality of stylistics is its potential to spark
378
<PAGE>
Further reading
off interest in areas which are of less central concern for many readers, but which have the potential to illuminate literary style in new ways. The following is a 'mixed bag' of publications inviting the engagement of the reader in a variety of specific ways:
ALLEN, G. (2000) Intertextuality, Routledge.
BELLARD-THOMSON, C. (1992) The Literary Stylistics of French, Manchester, Manchester University Press.
CARTER, R. (2004) Language and Creativity, Routledge.
CULPEPER, J. (2001) Language and Characterisation: people in plays and other texts, Longman.

P. Pedagogical stylistics, including textbooks for students
In the study of language-in-literature, pedagogy has always had a high priority, whether in the teaching of native-speaker or second-language students of English. The following list includes introductory textbooks for students, as well as publications expounding pedagogical issues and methods. Language and Style is a web-based introductory stylistics course which is freely available, and Language and Literature 15(3) describes pedagogical investigations based on it. Carter (1982), listed under section A above, is also relevant here.
CARTER, R. and LONG, M.N. (1987) The Web of Words: exploring literature through language (Student and Teacher books), Cambridge University Press.
CARTER, R. and MCRAE, J. (1996) Language, Literature and the Learner: creative classroom practice, Longman.
HUGHES, R. (1996) English in Speech and Writing: investigating language and literature, Routledge.
LANGUAGE AND LITERATURE (2006) 15(3) Special Issue: The Language and Style Pedagogical Investigations.
LANGUAGE AND STYLE (www.lancs.ac.uk/fass/projects/stylistics/start.htm).
SANGER, K. (1998) The Language of Fiction, Routledge.
SHORT, M. (1983 [1982]) 'Stylistics and the teaching of literature: with an example from Joyce's Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man', in Brumfit, C.J. ed., Teaching Literature Overseas: language-based approaches, ELT Document 115, Pergamon Press and the British Council.

<PAGE>
Stylistics is the study of language in the service of literary ends, and in Style in Fiction Geoffrey Leech and Mick Short demonstrate how stylistic analysis can be applied novels and stories. Writing for both students of English language and English literature, they show the practical ways in which linguistic analysis and literary appreciation can be combined, and illuminated, through the study of literary style. Drawing mainly on major works of fiction of the last 150 years, insightful examination of style through texts and extracts is outstanding and of how prose writers achieve their effects that
Since its first publication in 1981, Style in Fiction has established textbook in its field, selling nearly 30,000 copies. Now, in the authors have added substantial new material, including two concluding chapters. These provide an extensive, up-to-date in the field over the past 25 years, and apply the methods in chapters to an analysis of an entire short story. The further the bibliographical references have also been thoroughly up

![](images/page_19_2.jpg)

Awarded the 2005 PALA Prize as the most influential book in stylistics published since 1980, Style in Fiction remains a classic guide to its discipline.
Professor Geoffrey Leech is Emeritus Professor of Linguistics and English Language at Lancaster University. He has written, co-authored or co-edited over 25 books, including A Glossary of English Grammar (2006) and Longman Grammar of Spoker and Written English (1999). Professor Mick Short is Professor of English Language and Literature at Lancaster University. He is the author of, among others, Corpus Stylistics: Speech, Writing and Thought Presentation in a Corpus of English Writing (2004) and Exploring the Language of Poems, Plays and Prose (1996).
